speeches.speech: Good day, sir.
lines: Good day, sir.

speeches.speech: I am glad you're well.
lines: I am glad you're well.

speeches.speech: I have not seen you long: how goes the world?
lines: I have not seen you long: how goes the world?

speeches.speech: It wears, sir, as it grows.
lines: It wears, sir, as it grows.

speeches.speech: Ay, that's well known: But what particular rarity? what strange, Which manifold record not matches? See, Magic of bounty! all these spirits thy power Hath conjured to attend. I know the merchant.
lines: Ay, that's well known:
But what particular rarity? what strange,
Which manifold record not matches? See,
Magic of bounty! all these spirits thy power
Hath conjured to attend. I know the merchant.

speeches.speech: I know them both; th' other's a jeweller.
lines: I know them both; th' other's a jeweller.

speeches.speech: O, 'tis a worthy lord.
lines: O, 'tis a worthy lord.

speeches.speech: Nay, that's most fix'd.
lines: Nay, that's most fix'd.

speeches.speech: A most incomparable man, breathed, as it were, To an untirable and continuate goodness: He passes.
lines: A most incomparable man, breathed, as it were,
To an untirable and continuate goodness:
He passes.

speeches.speech: O, pray, let's see't: for the Lord Timon, sir?
lines: O, pray, let's see't: for the Lord Timon, sir?

speeches.speech: [Reciting to himself] 'When we for recompense have praised the vile, It stains the glory in that happy verse Which aptly sings the good.'
lines: [Reciting to himself] 'When we for recompense have
praised the vile,
It stains the glory in that happy verse
Which aptly sings the good.'

speeches.speech: 'Tis a good form.
lines: 'Tis a good form.

speeches.speech: And rich: here is a water, look ye.
lines: And rich: here is a water, look ye.

speeches.speech: You are rapt, sir, in some work, some dedication To the great lord.
lines: You are rapt, sir, in some work, some dedication
To the great lord.

speeches.speech: A thing slipp'd idly from me. Our poesy is as a gum, which oozes From whence 'tis nourish'd: the fire i' the flint Shows not till it be struck; our gentle flame Provokes itself and like the current flies Each bound it chafes. What have you there?
lines: A thing slipp'd idly from me.
Our poesy is as a gum, which oozes
From whence 'tis nourish'd: the fire i' the flint
Shows not till it be struck; our gentle flame
Provokes itself and like the current flies
Each bound it chafes. What have you there?

speeches.speech: A picture, sir. When comes your book forth?
lines: A picture, sir. When comes your book forth?

speeches.speech: Upon the heels of my presentment, sir. Let's see your piece.
lines: Upon the heels of my presentment, sir.
Let's see your piece.

speeches.speech: 'Tis a good piece.
lines: 'Tis a good piece.

speeches.speech: So 'tis: this comes off well and excellent.
lines: So 'tis: this comes off well and excellent.

speeches.speech: Indifferent.
lines: Indifferent.

speeches.speech: Admirable: how this grace Speaks his own standing! what a mental power This eye shoots forth! how big imagination Moves in this lip! to the dumbness of the gesture One might interpret.
lines: Admirable: how this grace
Speaks his own standing! what a mental power
This eye shoots forth! how big imagination
Moves in this lip! to the dumbness of the gesture
One might interpret.

speeches.speech: It is a pretty mocking of the life. Here is a touch; is't good?
lines: It is a pretty mocking of the life.
Here is a touch; is't good?

speeches.speech: I will say of it, It tutors nature: artificial strife Lives in these touches, livelier than life.
lines: I will say of it,
It tutors nature: artificial strife
Lives in these touches, livelier than life.

speeches.speech: How this lord is follow'd!
lines: How this lord is follow'd!

speeches.speech: The senators of Athens: happy man!
lines: The senators of Athens: happy man!

speeches.speech: Look, more!
lines: Look, more!

speeches.speech: You see this confluence, this great flood of visitors. I have, in this rough work, shaped out a man, Whom this beneath world doth embrace and hug With amplest entertainment: my free drift Halts not particularly, but moves itself In a wide sea of wax: no levell'd malice Infects one comma in the course I hold; But flies an eagle flight, bold and forth on, Leaving no tract behind.
lines: You see this confluence, this great flood
of visitors.
I have, in this rough work, shaped out a man,
Whom this beneath world doth embrace and hug
With amplest entertainment: my free drift
Halts not particularly, but moves itself
In a wide sea of wax: no levell'd malice
Infects one comma in the course I hold;
But flies an eagle flight, bold and forth on,
Leaving no tract behind.

speeches.speech: How shall I understand you?
lines: How shall I understand you?

speeches.speech: I will unbolt to you. You see how all conditions, how all minds, As well of glib and slippery creatures as Of grave and austere quality, tender down Their services to Lord Timon: his large fortune Upon his good and gracious nature hanging Subdues and properties to his love and tendance All sorts of hearts; yea, from the glass-faced flatterer To Apemantus, that few things loves better Than to abhor himself: even he drops down The knee before him, and returns in peace Most rich in Timon's nod.
lines: I will unbolt to you.
You see how all conditions, how all minds,
As well of glib and slippery creatures as
Of grave and austere quality, tender down
Their services to Lord Timon: his large fortune
Upon his good and gracious nature hanging
Subdues and properties to his love and tendance
All sorts of hearts; yea, from the glass-faced flatterer
To Apemantus, that few things loves better
Than to abhor himself: even he drops down
The knee before him, and returns in peace
Most rich in Timon's nod.

speeches.speech: I saw them speak together.
lines: I saw them speak together.

speeches.speech: Sir, I have upon a high and pleasant hill Feign'd Fortune to be throned: the base o' the mount Is rank'd with all deserts, all kind of natures, That labour on the bosom of this sphere To propagate their states: amongst them all, Whose eyes are on this sovereign lady fix'd, One do I personate of Lord Timon's frame, Whom Fortune with her ivory hand wafts to her; Whose present grace to present slaves and servants Translates his rivals.
lines: Sir, I have upon a high and pleasant hill
Feign'd Fortune to be throned: the base o' the mount
Is rank'd with all deserts, all kind of natures,
That labour on the bosom of this sphere
To propagate their states: amongst them all,
Whose eyes are on this sovereign lady fix'd,
One do I personate of Lord Timon's frame,
Whom Fortune with her ivory hand wafts to her;
Whose present grace to present slaves and servants
Translates his rivals.

speeches.speech: 'Tis conceived to scope. This throne, this Fortune, and this hill, methinks, With one man beckon'd from the rest below, Bowing his head against the sleepy mount To climb his happiness, would be well express'd In our condition.
lines: 'Tis conceived to scope.
This throne, this Fortune, and this hill, methinks,
With one man beckon'd from the rest below,
Bowing his head against the sleepy mount
To climb his happiness, would be well express'd
In our condition.

speeches.speech: Nay, sir, but hear me on. All those which were his fellows but of late, Some better than his value, on the moment Follow his strides, his lobbies fill with tendance, Rain sacrificial whisperings in his ear, Make sacred even his stirrup, and through him Drink the free air.
lines: Nay, sir, but hear me on.
All those which were his fellows but of late,
Some better than his value, on the moment
Follow his strides, his lobbies fill with tendance,
Rain sacrificial whisperings in his ear,
Make sacred even his stirrup, and through him
Drink the free air.

speeches.speech: Ay, marry, what of these?
lines: Ay, marry, what of these?

speeches.speech: When Fortune in her shift and change of mood Spurns down her late beloved, all his dependants Which labour'd after him to the mountain's top Even on their knees and hands, let him slip down, Not one accompanying his declining foot.
lines: When Fortune in her shift and change of mood
Spurns down her late beloved, all his dependants
Which labour'd after him to the mountain's top
Even on their knees and hands, let him slip down,
Not one accompanying his declining foot.

speeches.speech: 'Tis common: A thousand moral paintings I can show That shall demonstrate these quick blows of Fortune's More pregnantly than words. Yet you do well To show Lord Timon that mean eyes have seen The foot above the head. [Trumpets sound. Enter TIMON, addressing himself] courteously to every suitor; a Messenger from VENTIDIUS talking with him; LUCILIUS and other servants following]
lines: 'Tis common:
A thousand moral paintings I can show
That shall demonstrate these quick blows of Fortune's
More pregnantly than words. Yet you do well
To show Lord Timon that mean eyes have seen
The foot above the head.
[Trumpets sound. Enter TIMON, addressing himself]
courteously to every suitor; a Messenger from
VENTIDIUS talking with him; LUCILIUS and other
servants following]

speeches.speech: Imprison'd is he, say you?
lines: Imprison'd is he, say you?

speeches.speech: Ay, my good lord: five talents is his debt, His means most short, his creditors most strait: Your honourable letter he desires To those have shut him up; which failing, Periods his comfort.
lines: Ay, my good lord: five talents is his debt,
His means most short, his creditors most strait:
Your honourable letter he desires
To those have shut him up; which failing,
Periods his comfort.

speeches.speech: Noble Ventidius! Well; I am not of that feather to shake off My friend when he must need me. I do know him A gentleman that well deserves a help: Which he shall have: I'll pay the debt, and free him.
lines: Noble Ventidius! Well;
I am not of that feather to shake off
My friend when he must need me. I do know him
A gentleman that well deserves a help:
Which he shall have: I'll pay the debt,
and free him.

speeches.speech: Your lordship ever binds him.
lines: Your lordship ever binds him.

speeches.speech: Commend me to him: I will send his ransom; And being enfranchised, bid him come to me. 'Tis not enough to help the feeble up, But to support him after. Fare you well.
lines: Commend me to him: I will send his ransom;
And being enfranchised, bid him come to me.
'Tis not enough to help the feeble up,
But to support him after. Fare you well.

speeches.speech: All happiness to your honour!
lines: All happiness to your honour!

speeches.speech: Lord Timon, hear me speak.
lines: Lord Timon, hear me speak.

speeches.speech: Freely, good father.
lines: Freely, good father.

speeches.speech: Thou hast a servant named Lucilius.
lines: Thou hast a servant named Lucilius.

speeches.speech: I have so: what of him?
lines: I have so: what of him?

speeches.speech: Most noble Timon, call the man before thee.
lines: Most noble Timon, call the man before thee.

speeches.speech: Attends he here, or no? Lucilius!
lines: Attends he here, or no? Lucilius!

speeches.speech: Here, at your lordship's service.
lines: Here, at your lordship's service.

speeches.speech: This fellow here, Lord Timon, this thy creature, By night frequents my house. I am a man That from my first have been inclined to thrift; And my estate deserves an heir more raised Than one which holds a trencher.
lines: This fellow here, Lord Timon, this thy creature,
By night frequents my house. I am a man
That from my first have been inclined to thrift;
And my estate deserves an heir more raised
Than one which holds a trencher.

speeches.speech: Well; what further?
lines: Well; what further?

speeches.speech: One only daughter have I, no kin else, On whom I may confer what I have got: The maid is fair, o' the youngest for a bride, And I have bred her at my dearest cost In qualities of the best. This man of thine Attempts her love: I prithee, noble lord, Join with me to forbid him her resort; Myself have spoke in vain.
lines: One only daughter have I, no kin else,
On whom I may confer what I have got:
The maid is fair, o' the youngest for a bride,
And I have bred her at my dearest cost
In qualities of the best. This man of thine
Attempts her love: I prithee, noble lord,
Join with me to forbid him her resort;
Myself have spoke in vain.

speeches.speech: The man is honest.
lines: The man is honest.

speeches.speech: Therefore he will be, Timon: His honesty rewards him in itself; It must not bear my daughter.
lines: Therefore he will be, Timon:
His honesty rewards him in itself;
It must not bear my daughter.

speeches.speech: Does she love him?
lines: Does she love him?

speeches.speech: She is young and apt: Our own precedent passions do instruct us What levity's in youth.
lines: She is young and apt:
Our own precedent passions do instruct us
What levity's in youth.

speeches.speech: [To LUCILIUS] Love you the maid?
lines: [To LUCILIUS] Love you the maid?

speeches.speech: Ay, my good lord, and she accepts of it.
lines: Ay, my good lord, and she accepts of it.

speeches.speech: If in her marriage my consent be missing, I call the gods to witness, I will choose Mine heir from forth the beggars of the world, And dispossess her all.
lines: If in her marriage my consent be missing,
I call the gods to witness, I will choose
Mine heir from forth the beggars of the world,
And dispossess her all.

speeches.speech: How shall she be endow'd, if she be mated with an equal husband?
lines: How shall she be endow'd,
if she be mated with an equal husband?

speeches.speech: Three talents on the present; in future, all.
lines: Three talents on the present; in future, all.

speeches.speech: This gentleman of mine hath served me long: To build his fortune I will strain a little, For 'tis a bond in men. Give him thy daughter: What you bestow, in him I'll counterpoise, And make him weigh with her.
lines: This gentleman of mine hath served me long:
To build his fortune I will strain a little,
For 'tis a bond in men. Give him thy daughter:
What you bestow, in him I'll counterpoise,
And make him weigh with her.

speeches.speech: Most noble lord, Pawn me to this your honour, she is his.
lines: Most noble lord,
Pawn me to this your honour, she is his.

speeches.speech: My hand to thee; mine honour on my promise.
lines: My hand to thee; mine honour on my promise.

speeches.speech: Humbly I thank your lordship: never may The state or fortune fall into my keeping, Which is not owed to you!
lines: Humbly I thank your lordship: never may
The state or fortune fall into my keeping,
Which is not owed to you!

speeches.speech: Vouchsafe my labour, and long live your lordship!
lines: Vouchsafe my labour, and long live your lordship!

speeches.speech: I thank you; you shall hear from me anon: Go not away. What have you there, my friend?
lines: I thank you; you shall hear from me anon:
Go not away. What have you there, my friend?

speeches.speech: A piece of painting, which I do beseech Your lordship to accept.
lines: A piece of painting, which I do beseech
Your lordship to accept.

speeches.speech: Painting is welcome. The painting is almost the natural man; or since dishonour traffics with man's nature, He is but outside: these pencill'd figures are Even such as they give out. I like your work; And you shall find I like it: wait attendance Till you hear further from me.
lines: Painting is welcome.
The painting is almost the natural man;
or since dishonour traffics with man's nature,
He is but outside: these pencill'd figures are
Even such as they give out. I like your work;
And you shall find I like it: wait attendance
Till you hear further from me.

speeches.speech: The gods preserve ye!
lines: The gods preserve ye!

speeches.speech: Well fare you, gentleman: give me your hand; We must needs dine together. Sir, your jewel Hath suffer'd under praise.
lines: Well fare you, gentleman: give me your hand;
We must needs dine together. Sir, your jewel
Hath suffer'd under praise.

speeches.speech: What, my lord! dispraise?
lines: What, my lord! dispraise?

speeches.speech: A more satiety of commendations. If I should pay you for't as 'tis extoll'd, It would unclew me quite.
lines: A more satiety of commendations.
If I should pay you for't as 'tis extoll'd,
It would unclew me quite.

speeches.speech: My lord, 'tis rated As those which sell would give: but you well know, Things of like value differing in the owners Are prized by their masters: believe't, dear lord, You mend the jewel by the wearing it.
lines: My lord, 'tis rated
As those which sell would give: but you well know,
Things of like value differing in the owners
Are prized by their masters: believe't, dear lord,
You mend the jewel by the wearing it.

speeches.speech: Well mock'd.
lines: Well mock'd.

speeches.speech: No, my good lord; he speaks the common tongue, Which all men speak with him.
lines: No, my good lord; he speaks the common tongue,
Which all men speak with him.

speeches.speech: Look, who comes here: will you be chid?
lines: Look, who comes here: will you be chid?

speeches.speech: He'll spare none.
lines: He'll spare none.

speeches.speech: Good morrow to thee, gentle Apemantus!
lines: Good morrow to thee, gentle Apemantus!

speeches.speech: Till I be gentle, stay thou for thy good morrow; When thou art Timon's dog, and these knaves honest.
lines: Till I be gentle, stay thou for thy good morrow;
When thou art Timon's dog, and these knaves honest.

speeches.speech: Why dost thou call them knaves? thou know'st them not.
lines: Why dost thou call them knaves? thou know'st them not.

speeches.speech: Are they not Athenians?
lines: Are they not Athenians?

speeches.speech: Yes.
lines: Yes.

speeches.speech: Then I repent not.
lines: Then I repent not.

speeches.speech: Thou know'st I do: I call'd thee by thy name.
lines: Thou know'st I do: I call'd thee by thy name.

speeches.speech: Thou art proud, Apemantus.
lines: Thou art proud, Apemantus.

speeches.speech: Of nothing so much as that I am not like Timon.
lines: Of nothing so much as that I am not like Timon.

speeches.speech: Whither art going?
lines: Whither art going?

speeches.speech: To knock out an honest Athenian's brains.
lines: To knock out an honest Athenian's brains.

speeches.speech: That's a deed thou'lt die for.
lines: That's a deed thou'lt die for.

speeches.speech: Right, if doing nothing be death by the law.
lines: Right, if doing nothing be death by the law.

speeches.speech: How likest thou this picture, Apemantus?
lines: How likest thou this picture, Apemantus?

speeches.speech: The best, for the innocence.
lines: The best, for the innocence.

speeches.speech: Wrought he not well that painted it?
lines: Wrought he not well that painted it?

speeches.speech: He wrought better that made the painter; and yet he's but a filthy piece of work.
lines: He wrought better that made the painter; and yet
he's but a filthy piece of work.

speeches.speech: You're a dog.
lines: You're a dog.

speeches.speech: Thy mother's of my generation: what's she, if I be a dog?
lines: Thy mother's of my generation: what's she, if I be a dog?

speeches.speech: Wilt dine with me, Apemantus?
lines: Wilt dine with me, Apemantus?

speeches.speech: No; I eat not lords.
lines: No; I eat not lords.

speeches.speech: An thou shouldst, thou 'ldst anger ladies.
lines: An thou shouldst, thou 'ldst anger ladies.

speeches.speech: O, they eat lords; so they come by great bellies.
lines: O, they eat lords; so they come by great bellies.

speeches.speech: That's a lascivious apprehension.
lines: That's a lascivious apprehension.

speeches.speech: So thou apprehendest it: take it for thy labour.
lines: So thou apprehendest it: take it for thy labour.

speeches.speech: How dost thou like this jewel, Apemantus?
lines: How dost thou like this jewel, Apemantus?

speeches.speech: Not so well as plain-dealing, which will not cost a man a doit.
lines: Not so well as plain-dealing, which will not cost a
man a doit.

speeches.speech: What dost thou think 'tis worth?
lines: What dost thou think 'tis worth?

speeches.speech: Not worth my thinking. How now, poet!
lines: Not worth my thinking. How now, poet!

speeches.speech: How now, philosopher!
lines: How now, philosopher!

speeches.speech: Thou liest.
lines: Thou liest.

speeches.speech: Art not one?
lines: Art not one?

speeches.speech: Yes.
lines: Yes.

speeches.speech: Then I lie not.
lines: Then I lie not.

speeches.speech: Art not a poet?
lines: Art not a poet?

speeches.speech: Yes.
lines: Yes.

speeches.speech: Then thou liest: look in thy last work, where thou hast feigned him a worthy fellow.
lines: Then thou liest: look in thy last work, where thou
hast feigned him a worthy fellow.

speeches.speech: That's not feigned; he is so.
lines: That's not feigned; he is so.

speeches.speech: Yes, he is worthy of thee, and to pay thee for thy labour: he that loves to be flattered is worthy o' the flatterer. Heavens, that I were a lord!
lines: Yes, he is worthy of thee, and to pay thee for thy
labour: he that loves to be flattered is worthy o'
the flatterer. Heavens, that I were a lord!

speeches.speech: What wouldst do then, Apemantus?
lines: What wouldst do then, Apemantus?

speeches.speech: E'en as Apemantus does now; hate a lord with my heart.
lines: E'en as Apemantus does now; hate a lord with my heart.

speeches.speech: What, thyself?
lines: What, thyself?

speeches.speech: Ay.
lines: Ay.

speeches.speech: Wherefore?
lines: Wherefore?

speeches.speech: That I had no angry wit to be a lord. Art not thou a merchant?
lines: That I had no angry wit to be a lord.
Art not thou a merchant?

speeches.speech: Ay, Apemantus.
lines: Ay, Apemantus.

speeches.speech: Traffic confound thee, if the gods will not!
lines: Traffic confound thee, if the gods will not!

speeches.speech: If traffic do it, the gods do it.
lines: If traffic do it, the gods do it.

speeches.speech: Traffic's thy god; and thy god confound thee!
lines: Traffic's thy god; and thy god confound thee!

speeches.speech: What trumpet's that?
lines: What trumpet's that?

speeches.speech: 'Tis Alcibiades, and some twenty horse, All of companionship.
lines: 'Tis Alcibiades, and some twenty horse,
All of companionship.

speeches.speech: Pray, entertain them; give them guide to us. [Exeunt some Attendants] You must needs dine with me: go not you hence Till I have thank'd you: when dinner's done, Show me this piece. I am joyful of your sights. [Enter ALCIBIADES, with the rest] Most welcome, sir!
lines: Pray, entertain them; give them guide to us.
[Exeunt some Attendants]
You must needs dine with me: go not you hence
Till I have thank'd you: when dinner's done,
Show me this piece. I am joyful of your sights.
[Enter ALCIBIADES, with the rest]
Most welcome, sir!

speeches.speech: So, so, there! Aches contract and starve your supple joints! That there should be small love 'mongst these sweet knaves, And all this courtesy! The strain of man's bred out Into baboon and monkey.
lines: So, so, there!
Aches contract and starve your supple joints!
That there should be small love 'mongst these
sweet knaves,
And all this courtesy! The strain of man's bred out
Into baboon and monkey.

speeches.speech: Sir, you have saved my longing, and I feed Most hungerly on your sight.
lines: Sir, you have saved my longing, and I feed
Most hungerly on your sight.

speeches.speech: Right welcome, sir! Ere we depart, we'll share a bounteous time In different pleasures. Pray you, let us in.
lines: Right welcome, sir!
Ere we depart, we'll share a bounteous time
In different pleasures. Pray you, let us in.

speeches.speech: What time o' day is't, Apemantus?
lines: What time o' day is't, Apemantus?

speeches.speech: Time to be honest.
lines: Time to be honest.

speeches.speech: That time serves still.
lines: That time serves still.

speeches.speech: The more accursed thou, that still omitt'st it.
lines: The more accursed thou, that still omitt'st it.

speeches.speech: Thou art going to Lord Timon's feast?
lines: Thou art going to Lord Timon's feast?

speeches.speech: Ay, to see meat fill knaves and wine heat fools.
lines: Ay, to see meat fill knaves and wine heat fools.

speeches.speech: Fare thee well, fare thee well.
lines: Fare thee well, fare thee well.

speeches.speech: Thou art a fool to bid me farewell twice.
lines: Thou art a fool to bid me farewell twice.

speeches.speech: Why, Apemantus?
lines: Why, Apemantus?

speeches.speech: Shouldst have kept one to thyself, for I mean to give thee none.
lines: Shouldst have kept one to thyself, for I mean to
give thee none.

speeches.speech: Hang thyself!
lines: Hang thyself!

speeches.speech: No, I will do nothing at thy bidding: make thy requests to thy friend.
lines: No, I will do nothing at thy bidding: make thy
requests to thy friend.

speeches.speech: Away, unpeaceable dog, or I'll spurn thee hence!
lines: Away, unpeaceable dog, or I'll spurn thee hence!

speeches.speech: I will fly, like a dog, the heels o' the ass.
lines: I will fly, like a dog, the heels o' the ass.

speeches.speech: He's opposite to humanity. Come, shall we in, And taste Lord Timon's bounty? he outgoes The very heart of kindness.
lines: He's opposite to humanity. Come, shall we in,
And taste Lord Timon's bounty? he outgoes
The very heart of kindness.

speeches.speech: He pours it out; Plutus, the god of gold, Is but his steward: no meed, but he repays Sevenfold above itself; no gift to him, But breeds the giver a return exceeding All use of quittance.
lines: He pours it out; Plutus, the god of gold,
Is but his steward: no meed, but he repays
Sevenfold above itself; no gift to him,
But breeds the giver a return exceeding
All use of quittance.

speeches.speech: The noblest mind he carries That ever govern'd man.
lines: The noblest mind he carries
That ever govern'd man.

speeches.speech: Long may he live in fortunes! Shall we in?
lines: Long may he live in fortunes! Shall we in?

speeches.speech: I'll keep you company.
lines: I'll keep you company.

speeches.speech: Most honour'd Timon, It hath pleased the gods to remember my father's age, And call him to long peace. He is gone happy, and has left me rich: Then, as in grateful virtue I am bound To your free heart, I do return those talents, Doubled with thanks and service, from whose help I derived liberty.
lines: Most honour'd Timon,
It hath pleased the gods to remember my father's age,
And call him to long peace.
He is gone happy, and has left me rich:
Then, as in grateful virtue I am bound
To your free heart, I do return those talents,
Doubled with thanks and service, from whose help
I derived liberty.

speeches.speech: O, by no means, Honest Ventidius; you mistake my love: I gave it freely ever; and there's none Can truly say he gives, if he receives: If our betters play at that game, we must not dare To imitate them; faults that are rich are fair.
lines: O, by no means,
Honest Ventidius; you mistake my love:
I gave it freely ever; and there's none
Can truly say he gives, if he receives:
If our betters play at that game, we must not dare
To imitate them; faults that are rich are fair.

speeches.speech: A noble spirit!
lines: A noble spirit!

speeches.speech: Nay, my lords, [They all stand ceremoniously looking on TIMON] Ceremony was but devised at first To set a gloss on faint deeds, hollow welcomes, Recanting goodness, sorry ere 'tis shown; But where there is true friendship, there needs none. Pray, sit; more welcome are ye to my fortunes Than my fortunes to me.
lines: Nay, my lords,
[They all stand ceremoniously looking on TIMON]
Ceremony was but devised at first
To set a gloss on faint deeds, hollow welcomes,
Recanting goodness, sorry ere 'tis shown;
But where there is true friendship, there needs none.
Pray, sit; more welcome are ye to my fortunes
Than my fortunes to me.

speeches.speech: My lord, we always have confess'd it.
lines: My lord, we always have confess'd it.

speeches.speech: Ho, ho, confess'd it! hang'd it, have you not?
lines: Ho, ho, confess'd it! hang'd it, have you not?

speeches.speech: O, Apemantus, you are welcome.
lines: O, Apemantus, you are welcome.

speeches.speech: No; You shall not make me welcome: I come to have thee thrust me out of doors.
lines: No;
You shall not make me welcome:
I come to have thee thrust me out of doors.

speeches.speech: Fie, thou'rt a churl; ye've got a humour there Does not become a man: 'tis much to blame. They say, my lords, 'ira furor brevis est;' but yond man is ever angry. Go, let him have a table by himself, for he does neither affect company, nor is he fit for't, indeed.
lines: Fie, thou'rt a churl; ye've got a humour there
Does not become a man: 'tis much to blame.
They say, my lords, 'ira furor brevis est;' but yond
man is ever angry. Go, let him have a table by
himself, for he does neither affect company, nor is
he fit for't, indeed.

speeches.speech: Let me stay at thine apperil, Timon: I come to observe; I give thee warning on't.
lines: Let me stay at thine apperil, Timon: I come to
observe; I give thee warning on't.

speeches.speech: I take no heed of thee; thou'rt an Athenian, therefore welcome: I myself would have no power; prithee, let my meat make thee silent.
lines: I take no heed of thee; thou'rt an Athenian,
therefore welcome: I myself would have no power;
prithee, let my meat make thee silent.

speeches.speech: I scorn thy meat; 'twould choke me, for I should ne'er flatter thee. O you gods, what a number of men eat Timon, and he sees 'em not! It grieves me to see so many dip their meat in one man's blood; and all the madness is, he cheers them up too. I wonder men dare trust themselves with men: Methinks they should invite them without knives; Good for their meat, and safer for their lives. There's much example for't; the fellow that sits next him now, parts bread with him, pledges the breath of him in a divided draught, is the readiest man to kill him: 't has been proved. If I were a huge man, I should fear to drink at meals; Lest they should spy my windpipe's dangerous notes: Great men should drink with harness on their throats.
lines: I scorn thy meat; 'twould choke me, for I should
ne'er flatter thee. O you gods, what a number of
men eat Timon, and he sees 'em not! It grieves me
to see so many dip their meat in one man's blood;
and all the madness is, he cheers them up too.
I wonder men dare trust themselves with men:
Methinks they should invite them without knives;
Good for their meat, and safer for their lives.
There's much example for't; the fellow that sits
next him now, parts bread with him, pledges the
breath of him in a divided draught, is the readiest
man to kill him: 't has been proved. If I were a
huge man, I should fear to drink at meals;
Lest they should spy my windpipe's dangerous notes:
Great men should drink with harness on their throats.

speeches.speech: My lord, in heart; and let the health go round.
lines: My lord, in heart; and let the health go round.

speeches.speech: Let it flow this way, my good lord.
lines: Let it flow this way, my good lord.

speeches.speech: Flow this way! A brave fellow! he keeps his tides well. Those healths will make thee and thy state look ill, Timon. Here's that which is too weak to be a sinner, honest water, which ne'er left man i' the mire: This and my food are equals; there's no odds: Feasts are too proud to give thanks to the gods. Apemantus' grace. Immortal gods, I crave no pelf; I pray for no man but myself: Grant I may never prove so fond, To trust man on his oath or bond; Or a harlot, for her weeping; Or a dog, that seems a-sleeping: Or a keeper with my freedom; Or my friends, if I should need 'em. Amen. So fall to't: Rich men sin, and I eat root. [Eats and drinks] Much good dich thy good heart, Apemantus!
lines: Flow this way! A brave fellow! he keeps his tides
well. Those healths will make thee and thy state
look ill, Timon. Here's that which is too weak to
be a sinner, honest water, which ne'er left man i' the mire:
This and my food are equals; there's no odds:
Feasts are too proud to give thanks to the gods.
Apemantus' grace.
Immortal gods, I crave no pelf;
I pray for no man but myself:
Grant I may never prove so fond,
To trust man on his oath or bond;
Or a harlot, for her weeping;
Or a dog, that seems a-sleeping:
Or a keeper with my freedom;
Or my friends, if I should need 'em.
Amen. So fall to't:
Rich men sin, and I eat root.
[Eats and drinks]
Much good dich thy good heart, Apemantus!

speeches.speech: Captain Alcibiades, your heart's in the field now.
lines: Captain Alcibiades, your heart's in the field now.

speeches.speech: My heart is ever at your service, my lord.
lines: My heart is ever at your service, my lord.

speeches.speech: You had rather be at a breakfast of enemies than a dinner of friends.
lines: You had rather be at a breakfast of enemies than a
dinner of friends.

speeches.speech: So the were bleeding-new, my lord, there's no meat like 'em: I could wish my best friend at such a feast.
lines: So the were bleeding-new, my lord, there's no meat
like 'em: I could wish my best friend at such a feast.

speeches.speech: Would all those fatterers were thine enemies then, that then thou mightst kill 'em and bid me to 'em!
lines: Would all those fatterers were thine enemies then,
that then thou mightst kill 'em and bid me to 'em!

speeches.speech: Might we but have that happiness, my lord, that you would once use our hearts, whereby we might express some part of our zeals, we should think ourselves for ever perfect.
lines: Might we but have that happiness, my lord, that you
would once use our hearts, whereby we might express
some part of our zeals, we should think ourselves
for ever perfect.

speeches.speech: O, no doubt, my good friends, but the gods themselves have provided that I shall have much help from you: how had you been my friends else? why have you that charitable title from thousands, did not you chiefly belong to my heart? I have told more of you to myself than you can with modesty speak in your own behalf; and thus far I confirm you. O you gods, think I, what need we have any friends, if we should ne'er have need of 'em? they were the most needless creatures living, should we ne'er have use for 'em, and would most resemble sweet instruments hung up in cases that keep their sounds to themselves. Why, I have often wished myself poorer, that I might come nearer to you. We are born to do benefits: and what better or properer can we can our own than the riches of our friends? O, what a precious comfort 'tis, to have so many, like brothers, commanding one another's fortunes! O joy, e'en made away ere 't can be born! Mine eyes cannot hold out water, methinks: to forget their faults, I drink to you.
lines: O, no doubt, my good friends, but the gods
themselves have provided that I shall have much help
from you: how had you been my friends else? why
have you that charitable title from thousands, did
not you chiefly belong to my heart? I have told
more of you to myself than you can with modesty
speak in your own behalf; and thus far I confirm
you. O you gods, think I, what need we have any
friends, if we should ne'er have need of 'em? they
were the most needless creatures living, should we
ne'er have use for 'em, and would most resemble
sweet instruments hung up in cases that keep their
sounds to themselves. Why, I have often wished
myself poorer, that I might come nearer to you. We
are born to do benefits: and what better or
properer can we can our own than the riches of our
friends? O, what a precious comfort 'tis, to have
so many, like brothers, commanding one another's
fortunes! O joy, e'en made away ere 't can be born!
Mine eyes cannot hold out water, methinks: to
forget their faults, I drink to you.

speeches.speech: Thou weepest to make them drink, Timon.
lines: Thou weepest to make them drink, Timon.

speeches.speech: Joy had the like conception in our eyes And at that instant like a babe sprung up.
lines: Joy had the like conception in our eyes
And at that instant like a babe sprung up.

speeches.speech: Ho, ho! I laugh to think that babe a bastard.
lines: Ho, ho! I laugh to think that babe a bastard.

speeches.speech: I promise you, my lord, you moved me much.
lines: I promise you, my lord, you moved me much.

speeches.speech: Much!
lines: Much!

speeches.speech: What means that trump? [Enter a Servant] How now?
lines: What means that trump?
[Enter a Servant]
How now?

speeches.speech: Please you, my lord, there are certain ladies most desirous of admittance.
lines: Please you, my lord, there are certain
ladies most desirous of admittance.

speeches.speech: Ladies! what are their wills?
lines: Ladies! what are their wills?

speeches.speech: There comes with them a forerunner, my lord, which bears that office, to signify their pleasures.
lines: There comes with them a forerunner, my lord, which
bears that office, to signify their pleasures.

speeches.speech: I pray, let them be admitted.
lines: I pray, let them be admitted.

speeches.speech: Hail to thee, worthy Timon, and to all That of his bounties taste! The five best senses Acknowledge thee their patron; and come freely To gratulate thy plenteous bosom: th' ear, Taste, touch and smell, pleased from thy tale rise; They only now come but to feast thine eyes.
lines: Hail to thee, worthy Timon, and to all
That of his bounties taste! The five best senses
Acknowledge thee their patron; and come freely
To gratulate thy plenteous bosom: th' ear,
Taste, touch and smell, pleased from thy tale rise;
They only now come but to feast thine eyes.

speeches.speech: They're welcome all; let 'em have kind admittance: Music, make their welcome!
lines: They're welcome all; let 'em have kind admittance:
Music, make their welcome!

speeches.speech: You see, my lord, how ample you're beloved. [Music. Re-enter Cupid with a mask of Ladies] as Amazons, with lutes in their hands, dancing and playing]
lines: You see, my lord, how ample you're beloved.
[Music. Re-enter Cupid with a mask of Ladies]
as Amazons, with lutes in their hands,
dancing and playing]

speeches.speech: Hoy-day, what a sweep of vanity comes this way! They dance! they are mad women. Like madness is the glory of this life. As this pomp shows to a little oil and root. We make ourselves fools, to disport ourselves; And spend our flatteries, to drink those men Upon whose age we void it up again, With poisonous spite and envy. Who lives that's not depraved or depraves? Who dies, that bears not one spurn to their graves Of their friends' gift? I should fear those that dance before me now Would one day stamp upon me: 't has been done; Men shut their doors against a setting sun. [The Lords rise from table, with much adoring of] TIMON; and to show their loves, each singles out an Amazon, and all dance, men with women, a lofty strain or two to the hautboys, and cease]
lines: Hoy-day, what a sweep of vanity comes this way!
They dance! they are mad women.
Like madness is the glory of this life.
As this pomp shows to a little oil and root.
We make ourselves fools, to disport ourselves;
And spend our flatteries, to drink those men
Upon whose age we void it up again,
With poisonous spite and envy.
Who lives that's not depraved or depraves?
Who dies, that bears not one spurn to their graves
Of their friends' gift?
I should fear those that dance before me now
Would one day stamp upon me: 't has been done;
Men shut their doors against a setting sun.
[The Lords rise from table, with much adoring of]
TIMON; and to show their loves, each singles out an
Amazon, and all dance, men with women, a lofty
strain or two to the hautboys, and cease]

speeches.speech: You have done our pleasures much grace, fair ladies, Set a fair fashion on our entertainment, Which was not half so beautiful and kind; You have added worth unto 't and lustre, And entertain'd me with mine own device; I am to thank you for 't.
lines: You have done our pleasures much grace, fair ladies,
Set a fair fashion on our entertainment,
Which was not half so beautiful and kind;
You have added worth unto 't and lustre,
And entertain'd me with mine own device;
I am to thank you for 't.

speeches.speech: My lord, you take us even at the best.
lines: My lord, you take us even at the best.

speeches.speech: 'Faith, for the worst is filthy; and would not hold taking, I doubt me.
lines: 'Faith, for the worst is filthy; and would not hold
taking, I doubt me.

speeches.speech: Ladies, there is an idle banquet attends you: Please you to dispose yourselves.
lines: Ladies, there is an idle banquet attends you:
Please you to dispose yourselves.

speeches.speech: Most thankfully, my lord.
lines: Most thankfully, my lord.

speeches.speech: Flavius.
lines: Flavius.

speeches.speech: My lord?
lines: My lord?

speeches.speech: The little casket bring me hither.
lines: The little casket bring me hither.

speeches.speech: Yes, my lord. More jewels yet! There is no crossing him in 's humour; [Aside] Else I should tell him,--well, i' faith I should, When all's spent, he 'ld be cross'd then, an he could. 'Tis pity bounty had not eyes behind, That man might ne'er be wretched for his mind.
lines: Yes, my lord. More jewels yet!
There is no crossing him in 's humour;
[Aside]
Else I should tell him,--well, i' faith I should,
When all's spent, he 'ld be cross'd then, an he could.
'Tis pity bounty had not eyes behind,
That man might ne'er be wretched for his mind.

speeches.speech: Where be our men?
lines: Where be our men?

speeches.speech: Here, my lord, in readiness.
lines: Here, my lord, in readiness.

speeches.speech: Our horses!
lines: Our horses!

speeches.speech: O my friends, I have one word to say to you: look you, my good lord, I must entreat you, honour me so much As to advance this jewel; accept it and wear it, Kind my lord.
lines: O my friends,
I have one word to say to you: look you, my good lord,
I must entreat you, honour me so much
As to advance this jewel; accept it and wear it,
Kind my lord.

speeches.speech: I am so far already in your gifts,--
lines: I am so far already in your gifts,--

speeches.speech: So are we all.
lines: So are we all.

speeches.speech: My lord, there are certain nobles of the senate Newly alighted, and come to visit you.
lines: My lord, there are certain nobles of the senate
Newly alighted, and come to visit you.

speeches.speech: They are fairly welcome.
lines: They are fairly welcome.

speeches.speech: I beseech your honour, Vouchsafe me a word; it does concern you near.
lines: I beseech your honour,
Vouchsafe me a word; it does concern you near.

speeches.speech: Near! why then, another time I'll hear thee: I prithee, let's be provided to show them entertainment.
lines: Near! why then, another time I'll hear thee:
I prithee, let's be provided to show them
entertainment.

speeches.speech: [Aside] I scarce know how.
lines: [Aside] I scarce know how.

speeches.speech: May it please your honour, Lord Lucius, Out of his free love, hath presented to you Four milk-white horses, trapp'd in silver.
lines: May it please your honour, Lord Lucius,
Out of his free love, hath presented to you
Four milk-white horses, trapp'd in silver.

speeches.speech: I shall accept them fairly; let the presents Be worthily entertain'd. [Enter a third Servant] How now! what news?
lines: I shall accept them fairly; let the presents
Be worthily entertain'd.
[Enter a third Servant]
How now! what news?

speeches.speech: Please you, my lord, that honourable gentleman, Lord Lucullus, entreats your company to-morrow to hunt with him, and has sent your honour two brace of greyhounds.
lines: Please you, my lord, that honourable
gentleman, Lord Lucullus, entreats your company
to-morrow to hunt with him, and has sent your honour
two brace of greyhounds.

speeches.speech: I'll hunt with him; and let them be received, Not without fair reward.
lines: I'll hunt with him; and let them be received,
Not without fair reward.

speeches.speech: [Aside] What will this come to? He commands us to provide, and give great gifts, And all out of an empty coffer: Nor will he know his purse, or yield me this, To show him what a beggar his heart is, Being of no power to make his wishes good: His promises fly so beyond his state That what he speaks is all in debt; he owes For every word: he is so kind that he now Pays interest for 't; his land's put to their books. Well, would I were gently put out of office Before I were forced out! Happier is he that has no friend to feed Than such that do e'en enemies exceed. I bleed inwardly for my lord.
lines: [Aside] What will this come to?
He commands us to provide, and give great gifts,
And all out of an empty coffer:
Nor will he know his purse, or yield me this,
To show him what a beggar his heart is,
Being of no power to make his wishes good:
His promises fly so beyond his state
That what he speaks is all in debt; he owes
For every word: he is so kind that he now
Pays interest for 't; his land's put to their books.
Well, would I were gently put out of office
Before I were forced out!
Happier is he that has no friend to feed
Than such that do e'en enemies exceed.
I bleed inwardly for my lord.

speeches.speech: You do yourselves Much wrong, you bate too much of your own merits: Here, my lord, a trifle of our love.
lines: You do yourselves
Much wrong, you bate too much of your own merits:
Here, my lord, a trifle of our love.

speeches.speech: With more than common thanks I will receive it.
lines: With more than common thanks I will receive it.

speeches.speech: O, he's the very soul of bounty!
lines: O, he's the very soul of bounty!

speeches.speech: And now I remember, my lord, you gave Good words the other day of a bay courser I rode on: it is yours, because you liked it.
lines: And now I remember, my lord, you gave
Good words the other day of a bay courser
I rode on: it is yours, because you liked it.

speeches.speech: O, I beseech you, pardon me, my lord, in that.
lines: O, I beseech you, pardon me, my lord, in that.

speeches.speech: You may take my word, my lord; I know, no man Can justly praise but what he does affect: I weigh my friend's affection with mine own; I'll tell you true. I'll call to you.
lines: You may take my word, my lord; I know, no man
Can justly praise but what he does affect:
I weigh my friend's affection with mine own;
I'll tell you true. I'll call to you.

speeches.speech: O, none so welcome.
lines: O, none so welcome.

speeches.speech: I take all and your several visitations So kind to heart, 'tis not enough to give; Methinks, I could deal kingdoms to my friends, And ne'er be weary. Alcibiades, Thou art a soldier, therefore seldom rich; It comes in charity to thee: for all thy living Is 'mongst the dead, and all the lands thou hast Lie in a pitch'd field.
lines: I take all and your several visitations
So kind to heart, 'tis not enough to give;
Methinks, I could deal kingdoms to my friends,
And ne'er be weary. Alcibiades,
Thou art a soldier, therefore seldom rich;
It comes in charity to thee: for all thy living
Is 'mongst the dead, and all the lands thou hast
Lie in a pitch'd field.

speeches.speech: Ay, defiled land, my lord.
lines: Ay, defiled land, my lord.

speeches.speech: We are so virtuously bound--
lines: We are so virtuously bound--

speeches.speech: And so Am I to you.
lines: And so
Am I to you.

speeches.speech: So infinitely endear'd--
lines: So infinitely endear'd--

speeches.speech: All to you. Lights, more lights!
lines: All to you. Lights, more lights!

speeches.speech: The best of happiness, Honour and fortunes, keep with you, Lord Timon!
lines: The best of happiness,
Honour and fortunes, keep with you, Lord Timon!

speeches.speech: Ready for his friends.
lines: Ready for his friends.

speeches.speech: What a coil's here! Serving of becks and jutting-out of bums! I doubt whether their legs be worth the sums That are given for 'em. Friendship's full of dregs: Methinks, false hearts should never have sound legs, Thus honest fools lay out their wealth on court'sies.
lines: What a coil's here!
Serving of becks and jutting-out of bums!
I doubt whether their legs be worth the sums
That are given for 'em. Friendship's full of dregs:
Methinks, false hearts should never have sound legs,
Thus honest fools lay out their wealth on court'sies.

speeches.speech: Now, Apemantus, if thou wert not sullen, I would be good to thee.
lines: Now, Apemantus, if thou wert not sullen, I would be
good to thee.

speeches.speech: No, I'll nothing: for if I should be bribed too, there would be none left to rail upon thee, and then thou wouldst sin the faster. Thou givest so long, Timon, I fear me thou wilt give away thyself in paper shortly: what need these feasts, pomps and vain-glories?
lines: No, I'll nothing: for if I should be bribed too,
there would be none left to rail upon thee, and then
thou wouldst sin the faster. Thou givest so long,
Timon, I fear me thou wilt give away thyself in
paper shortly: what need these feasts, pomps and
vain-glories?

speeches.speech: Nay, an you begin to rail on society once, I am sworn not to give regard to you. Farewell; and come with better music.
lines: Nay, an you begin to rail on society once, I am
sworn not to give regard to you. Farewell; and come
with better music.

speeches.speech: So: Thou wilt not hear me now; thou shalt not then: I'll lock thy heaven from thee. O, that men's ears should be To counsel deaf, but not to flattery!
lines: So:
Thou wilt not hear me now; thou shalt not then:
I'll lock thy heaven from thee.
O, that men's ears should be
To counsel deaf, but not to flattery!

speeches.speech: And late, five thousand: to Varro and to Isidore He owes nine thousand; besides my former sum, Which makes it five and twenty. Still in motion Of raging waste? It cannot hold; it will not. If I want gold, steal but a beggar's dog, And give it Timon, why, the dog coins gold. If I would sell my horse, and buy twenty more Better than he, why, give my horse to Timon, Ask nothing, give it him, it foals me, straight, And able horses. No porter at his gate, But rather one that smiles and still invites All that pass by. It cannot hold: no reason Can found his state in safety. Caphis, ho! Caphis, I say!
lines: And late, five thousand: to Varro and to Isidore
He owes nine thousand; besides my former sum,
Which makes it five and twenty. Still in motion
Of raging waste? It cannot hold; it will not.
If I want gold, steal but a beggar's dog,
And give it Timon, why, the dog coins gold.
If I would sell my horse, and buy twenty more
Better than he, why, give my horse to Timon,
Ask nothing, give it him, it foals me, straight,
And able horses. No porter at his gate,
But rather one that smiles and still invites
All that pass by. It cannot hold: no reason
Can found his state in safety. Caphis, ho!
Caphis, I say!

speeches.speech: Here, sir; what is your pleasure?
lines: Here, sir; what is your pleasure?

speeches.speech: Get on your cloak, and haste you to Lord Timon; Importune him for my moneys; be not ceased With slight denial, nor then silenced when-- 'Commend me to your master'--and the cap Plays in the right hand, thus: but tell him, My uses cry to me, I must serve my turn Out of mine own; his days and times are past And my reliances on his fracted dates Have smit my credit: I love and honour him, But must not break my back to heal his finger; Immediate are my needs, and my relief Must not be toss'd and turn'd to me in words, But find supply immediate. Get you gone: Put on a most importunate aspect, A visage of demand; for, I do fear, When every feather sticks in his own wing, Lord Timon will be left a naked gull, Which flashes now a phoenix. Get you gone.
lines: Get on your cloak, and haste you to Lord Timon;
Importune him for my moneys; be not ceased
With slight denial, nor then silenced when--
'Commend me to your master'--and the cap
Plays in the right hand, thus: but tell him,
My uses cry to me, I must serve my turn
Out of mine own; his days and times are past
And my reliances on his fracted dates
Have smit my credit: I love and honour him,
But must not break my back to heal his finger;
Immediate are my needs, and my relief
Must not be toss'd and turn'd to me in words,
But find supply immediate. Get you gone:
Put on a most importunate aspect,
A visage of demand; for, I do fear,
When every feather sticks in his own wing,
Lord Timon will be left a naked gull,
Which flashes now a phoenix. Get you gone.

speeches.speech: I go, sir.
lines: I go, sir.

speeches.speech: 'I go, sir!'--Take the bonds along with you, And have the dates in contempt.
lines: 'I go, sir!'--Take the bonds along with you,
And have the dates in contempt.

speeches.speech: I will, sir.
lines: I will, sir.

speeches.speech: Go.
lines: Go.

speeches.speech: No care, no stop! so senseless of expense, That he will neither know how to maintain it, Nor cease his flow of riot: takes no account How things go from him, nor resumes no care Of what is to continue: never mind Was to be so unwise, to be so kind. What shall be done? he will not hear, till feel: I must be round with him, now he comes from hunting. Fie, fie, fie, fie!
lines: No care, no stop! so senseless of expense,
That he will neither know how to maintain it,
Nor cease his flow of riot: takes no account
How things go from him, nor resumes no care
Of what is to continue: never mind
Was to be so unwise, to be so kind.
What shall be done? he will not hear, till feel:
I must be round with him, now he comes from hunting.
Fie, fie, fie, fie!

speeches.speech: Good even, Varro: what, You come for money?
lines: Good even, Varro: what,
You come for money?

speeches.speech: It is: and yours too, Isidore?
lines: It is: and yours too, Isidore?

speeches.speech: Would we were all discharged!
lines: Would we were all discharged!

speeches.speech: Here comes the lord.
lines: Here comes the lord.

speeches.speech: So soon as dinner's done, we'll forth again, My Alcibiades. With me? what is your will?
lines: So soon as dinner's done, we'll forth again,
My Alcibiades. With me? what is your will?

speeches.speech: My lord, here is a note of certain dues.
lines: My lord, here is a note of certain dues.

speeches.speech: Dues! Whence are you?
lines: Dues! Whence are you?

speeches.speech: Of Athens here, my lord.
lines: Of Athens here, my lord.

speeches.speech: Go to my steward.
lines: Go to my steward.

speeches.speech: Please it your lordship, he hath put me off To the succession of new days this month: My master is awaked by great occasion To call upon his own, and humbly prays you That with your other noble parts you'll suit In giving him his right.
lines: Please it your lordship, he hath put me off
To the succession of new days this month:
My master is awaked by great occasion
To call upon his own, and humbly prays you
That with your other noble parts you'll suit
In giving him his right.

speeches.speech: Mine honest friend, I prithee, but repair to me next morning.
lines: Mine honest friend,
I prithee, but repair to me next morning.

speeches.speech: Nay, good my lord,--
lines: Nay, good my lord,--

speeches.speech: Contain thyself, good friend. He humbly prays your speedy payment.
lines: Contain thyself, good friend.
He humbly prays your speedy payment.

speeches.speech: If you did know, my lord, my master's wants-- And I am sent expressly to your lordship.
lines: If you did know, my lord, my master's wants--
And I am sent expressly to your lordship.

speeches.speech: Give me breath. I do beseech you, good my lords, keep on; I'll wait upon you instantly. [Exeunt ALCIBIADES and Lords] [To FLAVIUS] Come hither: pray you, How goes the world, that I am thus encounter'd With clamourous demands of date-broke bonds, And the detention of long-since-due debts, Against my honour?
lines: Give me breath.
I do beseech you, good my lords, keep on;
I'll wait upon you instantly.
[Exeunt ALCIBIADES and Lords]
[To FLAVIUS]
Come hither: pray you,
How goes the world, that I am thus encounter'd
With clamourous demands of date-broke bonds,
And the detention of long-since-due debts,
Against my honour?

speeches.speech: Please you, gentlemen, The time is unagreeable to this business: Your importunacy cease till after dinner, That I may make his lordship understand Wherefore you are not paid.
lines: Please you, gentlemen,
The time is unagreeable to this business:
Your importunacy cease till after dinner,
That I may make his lordship understand
Wherefore you are not paid.

speeches.speech: Do so, my friends. See them well entertain'd.
lines: Do so, my friends. See them well entertain'd.

speeches.speech: Pray, draw near.
lines: Pray, draw near.

speeches.speech: Stay, stay, here comes the fool with Apemantus: let's ha' some sport with 'em.
lines: Stay, stay, here comes the fool with Apemantus:
let's ha' some sport with 'em.

speeches.speech: Dost dialogue with thy shadow?
lines: Dost dialogue with thy shadow?

speeches.speech: No,'tis to thyself. [To the Fool] Come away.
lines: No,'tis to thyself.
[To the Fool]
Come away.

speeches.speech: No, thou stand'st single, thou'rt not on him yet.
lines: No, thou stand'st single, thou'rt not on him yet.

speeches.speech: Where's the fool now?
lines: Where's the fool now?

speeches.speech: He last asked the question. Poor rogues, and usurers' men! bawds between gold and want!
lines: He last asked the question. Poor rogues, and
usurers' men! bawds between gold and want!

speeches.speech: What are we, Apemantus?
lines: What are we, Apemantus?

speeches.speech: Asses.
lines: Asses.

speeches.speech: Why?
lines: Why?

speeches.speech: That you ask me what you are, and do not know yourselves. Speak to 'em, fool.
lines: That you ask me what you are, and do not know
yourselves. Speak to 'em, fool.

speeches.speech: How do you, gentlemen?
lines: How do you, gentlemen?

speeches.speech: Gramercies, good fool: how does your mistress?
lines: Gramercies, good fool: how does your mistress?

speeches.speech: She's e'en setting on water to scald such chickens as you are. Would we could see you at Corinth!
lines: She's e'en setting on water to scald such chickens
as you are. Would we could see you at Corinth!

speeches.speech: Good! gramercy.
lines: Good! gramercy.

speeches.speech: Look you, here comes my mistress' page.
lines: Look you, here comes my mistress' page.

speeches.speech: [To the Fool] Why, how now, captain! what do you in this wise company? How dost thou, Apemantus?
lines: [To the Fool] Why, how now, captain! what do you
in this wise company? How dost thou, Apemantus?

speeches.speech: Would I had a rod in my mouth, that I might answer thee profitably.
lines: Would I had a rod in my mouth, that I might answer
thee profitably.

speeches.speech: Prithee, Apemantus, read me the superscription of these letters: I know not which is which.
lines: Prithee, Apemantus, read me the superscription of
these letters: I know not which is which.

speeches.speech: Canst not read?
lines: Canst not read?

speeches.speech: No.
lines: No.

speeches.speech: There will little learning die then, that day thou art hanged. This is to Lord Timon; this to Alcibiades. Go; thou wast born a bastard, and thou't die a bawd.
lines: There will little learning die then, that day thou
art hanged. This is to Lord Timon; this to
Alcibiades. Go; thou wast born a bastard, and thou't
die a bawd.

speeches.speech: Thou wast whelped a dog, and thou shalt famish a dog's death. Answer not; I am gone.
lines: Thou wast whelped a dog, and thou shalt famish a
dog's death. Answer not; I am gone.

speeches.speech: E'en so thou outrunnest grace. Fool, I will go with you to Lord Timon's.
lines: E'en so thou outrunnest grace. Fool, I will go with
you to Lord Timon's.

speeches.speech: Will you leave me there?
lines: Will you leave me there?

speeches.speech: If Timon stay at home. You three serve three usurers?
lines: If Timon stay at home. You three serve three usurers?

speeches.speech: Ay; would they served us!
lines: Ay; would they served us!

speeches.speech: So would I,--as good a trick as ever hangman served thief.
lines: So would I,--as good a trick as ever hangman served thief.

speeches.speech: Are you three usurers' men?
lines: Are you three usurers' men?

speeches.speech: Ay, fool.
lines: Ay, fool.

speeches.speech: I think no usurer but has a fool to his servant: my mistress is one, and I am her fool. When men come to borrow of your masters, they approach sadly, and go away merry; but they enter my mistress' house merrily, and go away sadly: the reason of this?
lines: I think no usurer but has a fool to his servant: my
mistress is one, and I am her fool. When men come
to borrow of your masters, they approach sadly, and
go away merry; but they enter my mistress' house
merrily, and go away sadly: the reason of this?

speeches.speech: Do it then, that we may account thee a whoremaster and a knave; which not-withstanding, thou shalt be no less esteemed.
lines: Do it then, that we may account thee a whoremaster
and a knave; which not-withstanding, thou shalt be
no less esteemed.

speeches.speech: A fool in good clothes, and something like thee. 'Tis a spirit: sometime't appears like a lord; sometime like a lawyer; sometime like a philosopher, with two stones moe than's artificial one: he is very often like a knight; and, generally, in all shapes that man goes up and down in from fourscore to thirteen, this spirit walks in.
lines: A fool in good clothes, and something like thee.
'Tis a spirit: sometime't appears like a lord;
sometime like a lawyer; sometime like a philosopher,
with two stones moe than's artificial one: he is
very often like a knight; and, generally, in all
shapes that man goes up and down in from fourscore
to thirteen, this spirit walks in.

speeches.speech: Nor thou altogether a wise man: as much foolery as I have, so much wit thou lackest.
lines: Nor thou altogether a wise man: as much foolery as
I have, so much wit thou lackest.

speeches.speech: That answer might have become Apemantus.
lines: That answer might have become Apemantus.

speeches.speech: Aside, aside; here comes Lord Timon.
lines: Aside, aside; here comes Lord Timon.

speeches.speech: Come with me, fool, come.
lines: Come with me, fool, come.

speeches.speech: I do not always follow lover, elder brother and woman; sometime the philosopher.
lines: I do not always follow lover, elder brother and
woman; sometime the philosopher.

speeches.speech: Pray you, walk near: I'll speak with you anon.
lines: Pray you, walk near: I'll speak with you anon.

speeches.speech: You make me marvel: wherefore ere this time Had you not fully laid my state before me, That I might so have rated my expense, As I had leave of means?
lines: You make me marvel: wherefore ere this time
Had you not fully laid my state before me,
That I might so have rated my expense,
As I had leave of means?

speeches.speech: You would not hear me, At many leisures I proposed.
lines: You would not hear me,
At many leisures I proposed.

speeches.speech: Go to: Perchance some single vantages you took. When my indisposition put you back: And that unaptness made your minister, Thus to excuse yourself.
lines: Go to:
Perchance some single vantages you took.
When my indisposition put you back:
And that unaptness made your minister,
Thus to excuse yourself.

speeches.speech: O my good lord, At many times I brought in my accounts, Laid them before you; you would throw them off, And say, you found them in mine honesty. When, for some trifling present, you have bid me Return so much, I have shook my head and wept; Yea, 'gainst the authority of manners, pray'd you To hold your hand more close: I did endure Not seldom, nor no slight cheques, when I have Prompted you in the ebb of your estate And your great flow of debts. My loved lord, Though you hear now, too late--yet now's a time-- The greatest of your having lacks a half To pay your present debts.
lines: O my good lord,
At many times I brought in my accounts,
Laid them before you; you would throw them off,
And say, you found them in mine honesty.
When, for some trifling present, you have bid me
Return so much, I have shook my head and wept;
Yea, 'gainst the authority of manners, pray'd you
To hold your hand more close: I did endure
Not seldom, nor no slight cheques, when I have
Prompted you in the ebb of your estate
And your great flow of debts. My loved lord,
Though you hear now, too late--yet now's a time--
The greatest of your having lacks a half
To pay your present debts.

speeches.speech: Let all my land be sold.
lines: Let all my land be sold.

speeches.speech: 'Tis all engaged, some forfeited and gone; And what remains will hardly stop the mouth Of present dues: the future comes apace: What shall defend the interim? and at length How goes our reckoning?
lines: 'Tis all engaged, some forfeited and gone;
And what remains will hardly stop the mouth
Of present dues: the future comes apace:
What shall defend the interim? and at length
How goes our reckoning?

speeches.speech: To Lacedaemon did my land extend.
lines: To Lacedaemon did my land extend.

speeches.speech: O my good lord, the world is but a word: Were it all yours to give it in a breath, How quickly were it gone!
lines: O my good lord, the world is but a word:
Were it all yours to give it in a breath,
How quickly were it gone!

speeches.speech: You tell me true.
lines: You tell me true.

speeches.speech: If you suspect my husbandry or falsehood, Call me before the exactest auditors And set me on the proof. So the gods bless me, When all our offices have been oppress'd With riotous feeders, when our vaults have wept With drunken spilth of wine, when every room Hath blazed with lights and bray'd with minstrelsy, I have retired me to a wasteful cock, And set mine eyes at flow.
lines: If you suspect my husbandry or falsehood,
Call me before the exactest auditors
And set me on the proof. So the gods bless me,
When all our offices have been oppress'd
With riotous feeders, when our vaults have wept
With drunken spilth of wine, when every room
Hath blazed with lights and bray'd with minstrelsy,
I have retired me to a wasteful cock,
And set mine eyes at flow.

speeches.speech: Prithee, no more.
lines: Prithee, no more.

speeches.speech: Heavens, have I said, the bounty of this lord! How many prodigal bits have slaves and peasants This night englutted! Who is not Timon's? What heart, head, sword, force, means, but is Lord Timon's? Great Timon, noble, worthy, royal Timon! Ah, when the means are gone that buy this praise, The breath is gone whereof this praise is made: Feast-won, fast-lost; one cloud of winter showers, These flies are couch'd.
lines: Heavens, have I said, the bounty of this lord!
How many prodigal bits have slaves and peasants
This night englutted! Who is not Timon's?
What heart, head, sword, force, means, but is
Lord Timon's?
Great Timon, noble, worthy, royal Timon!
Ah, when the means are gone that buy this praise,
The breath is gone whereof this praise is made:
Feast-won, fast-lost; one cloud of winter showers,
These flies are couch'd.

speeches.speech: Come, sermon me no further: No villanous bounty yet hath pass'd my heart; Unwisely, not ignobly, have I given. Why dost thou weep? Canst thou the conscience lack, To think I shall lack friends? Secure thy heart; If I would broach the vessels of my love, And try the argument of hearts by borrowing, Men and men's fortunes could I frankly use As I can bid thee speak.
lines: Come, sermon me no further:
No villanous bounty yet hath pass'd my heart;
Unwisely, not ignobly, have I given.
Why dost thou weep? Canst thou the conscience lack,
To think I shall lack friends? Secure thy heart;
If I would broach the vessels of my love,
And try the argument of hearts by borrowing,
Men and men's fortunes could I frankly use
As I can bid thee speak.

speeches.speech: Assurance bless your thoughts!
lines: Assurance bless your thoughts!

speeches.speech: And, in some sort, these wants of mine are crown'd, That I account them blessings; for by these Shall I try friends: you shall perceive how you Mistake my fortunes; I am wealthy in my friends. Within there! Flaminius! Servilius!
lines: And, in some sort, these wants of mine are crown'd,
That I account them blessings; for by these
Shall I try friends: you shall perceive how you
Mistake my fortunes; I am wealthy in my friends.
Within there! Flaminius! Servilius!

speeches.speech: My lord? my lord?
lines: My lord? my lord?

speeches.speech: I will dispatch you severally; you to Lord Lucius; to Lord Lucullus you: I hunted with his honour to-day: you, to Sempronius: commend me to their loves, and, I am proud, say, that my occasions have found time to use 'em toward a supply of money: let the request be fifty talents.
lines: I will dispatch you severally; you to Lord Lucius;
to Lord Lucullus you: I hunted with his honour
to-day: you, to Sempronius: commend me to their
loves, and, I am proud, say, that my occasions have
found time to use 'em toward a supply of money: let
the request be fifty talents.

speeches.speech: As you have said, my lord.
lines: As you have said, my lord.

speeches.speech: [Aside] Lord Lucius and Lucullus? hum!
lines: [Aside] Lord Lucius and Lucullus? hum!

speeches.speech: Go you, sir, to the senators-- Of whom, even to the state's best health, I have Deserved this hearing--bid 'em send o' the instant A thousand talents to me.
lines: Go you, sir, to the senators--
Of whom, even to the state's best health, I have
Deserved this hearing--bid 'em send o' the instant
A thousand talents to me.

speeches.speech: I have been bold-- For that I knew it the most general way-- To them to use your signet and your name; But they do shake their heads, and I am here No richer in return.
lines: I have been bold--
For that I knew it the most general way--
To them to use your signet and your name;
But they do shake their heads, and I am here
No richer in return.

speeches.speech: Is't true? can't be?
lines: Is't true? can't be?

speeches.speech: They answer, in a joint and corporate voice, That now they are at fall, want treasure, cannot Do what they would; are sorry--you are honourable,-- But yet they could have wish'd--they know not-- Something hath been amiss--a noble nature May catch a wrench--would all were well--'tis pity;-- And so, intending other serious matters, After distasteful looks and these hard fractions, With certain half-caps and cold-moving nods They froze me into silence.
lines: They answer, in a joint and corporate voice,
That now they are at fall, want treasure, cannot
Do what they would; are sorry--you are honourable,--
But yet they could have wish'd--they know not--
Something hath been amiss--a noble nature
May catch a wrench--would all were well--'tis pity;--
And so, intending other serious matters,
After distasteful looks and these hard fractions,
With certain half-caps and cold-moving nods
They froze me into silence.

speeches.speech: You gods, reward them! Prithee, man, look cheerly. These old fellows Have their ingratitude in them hereditary: Their blood is caked, 'tis cold, it seldom flows; 'Tis lack of kindly warmth they are not kind; And nature, as it grows again toward earth, Is fashion'd for the journey, dull and heavy. [To a Servant] Go to Ventidius. [To FLAVIUS] Prithee, be not sad, Thou art true and honest; ingeniously I speak. No blame belongs to thee. [To Servant] Ventidius lately Buried his father; by whose death he's stepp'd Into a great estate: when he was poor, Imprison'd and in scarcity of friends, I clear'd him with five talents: greet him from me; Bid him suppose some good necessity Touches his friend, which craves to be remember'd With those five talents. [Exit Servant] [To FLAVIUS] That had, give't these fellows To whom 'tis instant due. Ne'er speak, or think, That Timon's fortunes 'mong his friends can sink.
lines: You gods, reward them!
Prithee, man, look cheerly. These old fellows
Have their ingratitude in them hereditary:
Their blood is caked, 'tis cold, it seldom flows;
'Tis lack of kindly warmth they are not kind;
And nature, as it grows again toward earth,
Is fashion'd for the journey, dull and heavy.
[To a Servant]
Go to Ventidius.
[To FLAVIUS]
Prithee, be not sad,
Thou art true and honest; ingeniously I speak.
No blame belongs to thee.
[To Servant]
Ventidius lately
Buried his father; by whose death he's stepp'd
Into a great estate: when he was poor,
Imprison'd and in scarcity of friends,
I clear'd him with five talents: greet him from me;
Bid him suppose some good necessity
Touches his friend, which craves to be remember'd
With those five talents.
[Exit Servant]
[To FLAVIUS]
That had, give't these fellows
To whom 'tis instant due. Ne'er speak, or think,
That Timon's fortunes 'mong his friends can sink.

speeches.speech: I would I could not think it: that thought is bounty's foe; Being free itself, it thinks all others so.
lines: I would I could not think it: that thought is
bounty's foe;
Being free itself, it thinks all others so.

speeches.speech: I have told my lord of you; he is coming down to you.
lines: I have told my lord of you; he is coming down to you.

speeches.speech: I thank you, sir.
lines: I thank you, sir.

speeches.speech: Here's my lord.
lines: Here's my lord.

speeches.speech: [Aside] One of Lord Timon's men? a gift, I warrant. Why, this hits right; I dreamt of a silver basin and ewer to-night. Flaminius, honest Flaminius; you are very respectively welcome, sir. Fill me some wine. [Exit Servants] And how does that honourable, complete, free-hearted gentleman of Athens, thy very bountiful good lord and master?
lines: [Aside] One of Lord Timon's men? a gift, I
warrant. Why, this hits right; I dreamt of a silver
basin and ewer to-night. Flaminius, honest
Flaminius; you are very respectively welcome, sir.
Fill me some wine.
[Exit Servants]
And how does that honourable, complete, free-hearted
gentleman of Athens, thy very bountiful good lord
and master?

speeches.speech: His health is well sir.
lines: His health is well sir.

speeches.speech: I am right glad that his health is well, sir: and what hast thou there under thy cloak, pretty Flaminius?
lines: I am right glad that his health is well, sir: and
what hast thou there under thy cloak, pretty Flaminius?

speeches.speech: 'Faith, nothing but an empty box, sir; which, in my lord's behalf, I come to entreat your honour to supply; who, having great and instant occasion to use fifty talents, hath sent to your lordship to furnish him, nothing doubting your present assistance therein.
lines: 'Faith, nothing but an empty box, sir; which, in my
lord's behalf, I come to entreat your honour to
supply; who, having great and instant occasion to
use fifty talents, hath sent to your lordship to
furnish him, nothing doubting your present
assistance therein.

speeches.speech: La, la, la, la! 'nothing doubting,' says he? Alas, good lord! a noble gentleman 'tis, if he would not keep so good a house. Many a time and often I ha' dined with him, and told him on't, and come again to supper to him, of purpose to have him spend less, and yet he would embrace no counsel, take no warning by my coming. Every man has his fault, and honesty is his: I ha' told him on't, but I could ne'er get him from't.
lines: La, la, la, la! 'nothing doubting,' says he? Alas,
good lord! a noble gentleman 'tis, if he would not
keep so good a house. Many a time and often I ha'
dined with him, and told him on't, and come again to
supper to him, of purpose to have him spend less,
and yet he would embrace no counsel, take no warning
by my coming. Every man has his fault, and honesty
is his: I ha' told him on't, but I could ne'er get
him from't.

speeches.speech: Please your lordship, here is the wine.
lines: Please your lordship, here is the wine.

speeches.speech: Flaminius, I have noted thee always wise. Here's to thee.
lines: Flaminius, I have noted thee always wise. Here's to thee.

speeches.speech: Your lordship speaks your pleasure.
lines: Your lordship speaks your pleasure.

speeches.speech: I have observed thee always for a towardly prompt spirit--give thee thy due--and one that knows what belongs to reason; and canst use the time well, if the time use thee well: good parts in thee. [To Servant] Get you gone, sirrah. [Exit Servant] Draw nearer, honest Flaminius. Thy lord's a bountiful gentleman: but thou art wise; and thou knowest well enough, although thou comest to me, that this is no time to lend money, especially upon bare friendship, without security. Here's three solidares for thee: good boy, wink at me, and say thou sawest me not. Fare thee well.
lines: I have observed thee always for a towardly prompt
spirit--give thee thy due--and one that knows what
belongs to reason; and canst use the time well, if
the time use thee well: good parts in thee.
[To Servant]
Get you gone, sirrah.
[Exit Servant]
Draw nearer, honest Flaminius. Thy lord's a
bountiful gentleman: but thou art wise; and thou
knowest well enough, although thou comest to me,
that this is no time to lend money, especially upon
bare friendship, without security. Here's three
solidares for thee: good boy, wink at me, and say
thou sawest me not. Fare thee well.

speeches.speech: Is't possible the world should so much differ, And we alive that lived? Fly, damned baseness, To him that worships thee!
lines: Is't possible the world should so much differ,
And we alive that lived? Fly, damned baseness,
To him that worships thee!

speeches.speech: Ha! now I see thou art a fool, and fit for thy master.
lines: Ha! now I see thou art a fool, and fit for thy master.

speeches.speech: May these add to the number that may scald thee! Let moulten coin be thy damnation, Thou disease of a friend, and not himself! Has friendship such a faint and milky heart, It turns in less than two nights? O you gods, I feel master's passion! this slave, Unto his honour, has my lord's meat in him: Why should it thrive and turn to nutriment, When he is turn'd to poison? O, may diseases only work upon't! And, when he's sick to death, let not that part of nature Which my lord paid for, be of any power To expel sickness, but prolong his hour!
lines: May these add to the number that may scald thee!
Let moulten coin be thy damnation,
Thou disease of a friend, and not himself!
Has friendship such a faint and milky heart,
It turns in less than two nights? O you gods,
I feel master's passion! this slave,
Unto his honour, has my lord's meat in him:
Why should it thrive and turn to nutriment,
When he is turn'd to poison?
O, may diseases only work upon't!
And, when he's sick to death, let not that part of nature
Which my lord paid for, be of any power
To expel sickness, but prolong his hour!

speeches.speech: Who, the Lord Timon? he is my very good friend, and an honourable gentleman.
lines: Who, the Lord Timon? he is my very good friend, and
an honourable gentleman.

speeches.speech: We know him for no less, though we are but strangers to him. But I can tell you one thing, my lord, and which I hear from common rumours: now Lord Timon's happy hours are done and past, and his estate shrinks from him.
lines: We know him for no less, though we are but strangers
to him. But I can tell you one thing, my lord, and
which I hear from common rumours: now Lord Timon's
happy hours are done and past, and his estate
shrinks from him.

speeches.speech: Fie, no, do not believe it; he cannot want for money.
lines: Fie, no, do not believe it; he cannot want for money.

speeches.speech: But believe you this, my lord, that, not long ago, one of his men was with the Lord Lucullus to borrow so many talents, nay, urged extremely for't and showed what necessity belonged to't, and yet was denied.
lines: But believe you this, my lord, that, not long ago,
