one of his men was with the Lord Lucullus to borrow
so many talents, nay, urged extremely for't and
showed what necessity belonged to't, and yet was denied.

speeches.speech: How!
lines: How!

speeches.speech: I tell you, denied, my lord.
lines: I tell you, denied, my lord.

speeches.speech: What a strange case was that! now, before the gods, I am ashamed on't. Denied that honourable man! there was very little honour showed in't. For my own part, I must needs confess, I have received some small kindnesses from him, as money, plate, jewels and such-like trifles, nothing comparing to his; yet, had he mistook him and sent to me, I should ne'er have denied his occasion so many talents.
lines: What a strange case was that! now, before the gods,
I am ashamed on't. Denied that honourable man!
there was very little honour showed in't. For my own
part, I must needs confess, I have received some
small kindnesses from him, as money, plate, jewels
and such-like trifles, nothing comparing to his;
yet, had he mistook him and sent to me, I should
ne'er have denied his occasion so many talents.

speeches.speech: See, by good hap, yonder's my lord; I have sweat to see his honour. My honoured lord,--
lines: See, by good hap, yonder's my lord;
I have sweat to see his honour. My honoured lord,--

speeches.speech: Servilius! you are kindly met, sir. Fare thee well: commend me to thy honourable virtuous lord, my very exquisite friend.
lines: Servilius! you are kindly met, sir. Fare thee well:
commend me to thy honourable virtuous lord, my very
exquisite friend.

speeches.speech: May it please your honour, my lord hath sent--
lines: May it please your honour, my lord hath sent--

speeches.speech: Ha! what has he sent? I am so much endeared to that lord; he's ever sending: how shall I thank him, thinkest thou? And what has he sent now?
lines: Ha! what has he sent? I am so much endeared to
that lord; he's ever sending: how shall I thank
him, thinkest thou? And what has he sent now?

speeches.speech: Has only sent his present occasion now, my lord; requesting your lordship to supply his instant use with so many talents.
lines: Has only sent his present occasion now, my lord;
requesting your lordship to supply his instant use
with so many talents.

speeches.speech: I know his lordship is but merry with me; He cannot want fifty five hundred talents.
lines: I know his lordship is but merry with me;
He cannot want fifty five hundred talents.

speeches.speech: But in the mean time he wants less, my lord. If his occasion were not virtuous, I should not urge it half so faithfully.
lines: But in the mean time he wants less, my lord.
If his occasion were not virtuous,
I should not urge it half so faithfully.

speeches.speech: Dost thou speak seriously, Servilius?
lines: Dost thou speak seriously, Servilius?

speeches.speech: Upon my soul,'tis true, sir.
lines: Upon my soul,'tis true, sir.

speeches.speech: What a wicked beast was I to disfurnish myself against such a good time, when I might ha' shown myself honourable! how unluckily it happened, that I should purchase the day before for a little part, and undo a great deal of honoured! Servilius, now, before the gods, I am not able to do,--the more beast, I say:--I was sending to use Lord Timon myself, these gentlemen can witness! but I would not, for the wealth of Athens, I had done't now. Commend me bountifully to his good lordship; and I hope his honour will conceive the fairest of me, because I have no power to be kind: and tell him this from me, I count it one of my greatest afflictions, say, that I cannot pleasure such an honourable gentleman. Good Servilius, will you befriend me so far, as to use mine own words to him?
lines: What a wicked beast was I to disfurnish myself
against such a good time, when I might ha' shown
myself honourable! how unluckily it happened, that I
should purchase the day before for a little part,
and undo a great deal of honoured! Servilius, now,
before the gods, I am not able to do,--the more
beast, I say:--I was sending to use Lord Timon
myself, these gentlemen can witness! but I would
not, for the wealth of Athens, I had done't now.
Commend me bountifully to his good lordship; and I
hope his honour will conceive the fairest of me,
because I have no power to be kind: and tell him
this from me, I count it one of my greatest
afflictions, say, that I cannot pleasure such an
honourable gentleman. Good Servilius, will you
befriend me so far, as to use mine own words to him?

speeches.speech: Yes, sir, I shall.
lines: Yes, sir, I shall.

speeches.speech: I'll look you out a good turn, Servilius. [Exit SERVILIUS] True as you said, Timon is shrunk indeed; And he that's once denied will hardly speed.
lines: I'll look you out a good turn, Servilius.
[Exit SERVILIUS]
True as you said, Timon is shrunk indeed;
And he that's once denied will hardly speed.

speeches.speech: Do you observe this, Hostilius?
lines: Do you observe this, Hostilius?

speeches.speech: Ay, too well.
lines: Ay, too well.

speeches.speech: Why, this is the world's soul; and just of the same piece Is every flatterer's spirit. Who can call him His friend that dips in the same dish? for, in My knowing, Timon has been this lord's father, And kept his credit with his purse, Supported his estate; nay, Timon's money Has paid his men their wages: he ne'er drinks, But Timon's silver treads upon his lip; And yet--O, see the monstrousness of man When he looks out in an ungrateful shape!-- He does deny him, in respect of his, What charitable men afford to beggars.
lines: Why, this is the world's soul; and just of the
same piece
Is every flatterer's spirit. Who can call him
His friend that dips in the same dish? for, in
My knowing, Timon has been this lord's father,
And kept his credit with his purse,
Supported his estate; nay, Timon's money
Has paid his men their wages: he ne'er drinks,
But Timon's silver treads upon his lip;
And yet--O, see the monstrousness of man
When he looks out in an ungrateful shape!--
He does deny him, in respect of his,
What charitable men afford to beggars.

speeches.speech: Religion groans at it.
lines: Religion groans at it.

speeches.speech: For mine own part, I never tasted Timon in my life, Nor came any of his bounties over me, To mark me for his friend; yet, I protest, For his right noble mind, illustrious virtue And honourable carriage, Had his necessity made use of me, I would have put my wealth into donation, And the best half should have return'd to him, So much I love his heart: but, I perceive, Men must learn now with pity to dispense; For policy sits above conscience.
lines: For mine own part,
I never tasted Timon in my life,
Nor came any of his bounties over me,
To mark me for his friend; yet, I protest,
For his right noble mind, illustrious virtue
And honourable carriage,
Had his necessity made use of me,
I would have put my wealth into donation,
And the best half should have return'd to him,
So much I love his heart: but, I perceive,
Men must learn now with pity to dispense;
For policy sits above conscience.

speeches.speech: Must he needs trouble me in 't,--hum!--'bove all others? He might have tried Lord Lucius or Lucullus; And now Ventidius is wealthy too, Whom he redeem'd from prison: all these Owe their estates unto him.
lines: Must he needs trouble me in 't,--hum!--'bove
all others?
He might have tried Lord Lucius or Lucullus;
And now Ventidius is wealthy too,
Whom he redeem'd from prison: all these
Owe their estates unto him.

speeches.speech: My lord, They have all been touch'd and found base metal, for They have au denied him.
lines: My lord,
They have all been touch'd and found base metal, for
They have au denied him.

speeches.speech: How! have they denied him? Has Ventidius and Lucullus denied him? And does he send to me? Three? hum! It shows but little love or judgment in him: Must I be his last refuge! His friends, like physicians, Thrive, give him over: must I take the cure upon me? Has much disgraced me in't; I'm angry at him, That might have known my place: I see no sense for't, But his occasion might have woo'd me first; For, in my conscience, I was the first man That e'er received gift from him: And does he think so backwardly of me now, That I'll requite its last? No: So it may prove an argument of laughter To the rest, and 'mongst lords I be thought a fool. I'ld rather than the worth of thrice the sum, Had sent to me first, but for my mind's sake; I'd such a courage to do him good. But now return, And with their faint reply this answer join; Who bates mine honour shall not know my coin.
lines: How! have they denied him?
Has Ventidius and Lucullus denied him?
And does he send to me? Three? hum!
It shows but little love or judgment in him:
Must I be his last refuge! His friends, like
physicians,
Thrive, give him over: must I take the cure upon me?
Has much disgraced me in't; I'm angry at him,
That might have known my place: I see no sense for't,
But his occasion might have woo'd me first;
For, in my conscience, I was the first man
That e'er received gift from him:
And does he think so backwardly of me now,
That I'll requite its last? No:
So it may prove an argument of laughter
To the rest, and 'mongst lords I be thought a fool.
I'ld rather than the worth of thrice the sum,
Had sent to me first, but for my mind's sake;
I'd such a courage to do him good. But now return,
And with their faint reply this answer join;
Who bates mine honour shall not know my coin.

speeches.speech: Excellent! Your lordship's a goodly villain. The devil knew not what he did when he made man politic; he crossed himself by 't: and I cannot think but, in the end, the villainies of man will set him clear. How fairly this lord strives to appear foul! takes virtuous copies to be wicked, like those that under hot ardent zeal would set whole realms on fire: Of such a nature is his politic love. This was my lord's best hope; now all are fled, Save only the gods: now his friends are dead, Doors, that were ne'er acquainted with their wards Many a bounteous year must be employ'd Now to guard sure their master. And this is all a liberal course allows; Who cannot keep his wealth must keep his house.
lines: Excellent! Your lordship's a goodly villain. The
devil knew not what he did when he made man
politic; he crossed himself by 't: and I cannot
think but, in the end, the villainies of man will
set him clear. How fairly this lord strives to
appear foul! takes virtuous copies to be wicked,
like those that under hot ardent zeal would set
whole realms on fire: Of such a nature is his
politic love.
This was my lord's best hope; now all are fled,
Save only the gods: now his friends are dead,
Doors, that were ne'er acquainted with their wards
Many a bounteous year must be employ'd
Now to guard sure their master.
And this is all a liberal course allows;
Who cannot keep his wealth must keep his house.

speeches.speech: Well met; good morrow, Titus and Hortensius.
lines: Well met; good morrow, Titus and Hortensius.

speeches.speech: The like to you kind Varro.
lines: The like to you kind Varro.

speeches.speech: Lucius! What, do we meet together? One business does command us all; for mine Is money.
lines: Lucius!
What, do we meet together?
One business does command us all; for mine Is money.

speeches.speech: So is theirs and ours.
lines: So is theirs and ours.

speeches.speech: Good day at once. What do you think the hour?
lines: Good day at once.
What do you think the hour?

speeches.speech: Labouring for nine.
lines: Labouring for nine.

speeches.speech: Is not my lord seen yet?
lines: Is not my lord seen yet?

speeches.speech: I wonder on't; he was wont to shine at seven. You must consider that a prodigal course Is like the sun's; but not, like his, recoverable. I fear 'tis deepest winter in Lord Timon's purse; That is one may reach deep enough, and yet Find little.
lines: I wonder on't; he was wont to shine at seven.
You must consider that a prodigal course
Is like the sun's; but not, like his, recoverable.
I fear 'tis deepest winter in Lord Timon's purse;
That is one may reach deep enough, and yet
Find little.

speeches.speech: I am of your fear for that.
lines: I am of your fear for that.

speeches.speech: I'll show you how to observe a strange event. Your lord sends now for money.
lines: I'll show you how to observe a strange event.
Your lord sends now for money.

speeches.speech: Most true, he does.
lines: Most true, he does.

speeches.speech: And he wears jewels now of Timon's gift, For which I wait for money.
lines: And he wears jewels now of Timon's gift,
For which I wait for money.

speeches.speech: It is against my heart. Timon in this should pay more than he owes: And e'en as if your lord should wear rich jewels, And send for money for 'em.
lines: It is against my heart.
Timon in this should pay more than he owes:
And e'en as if your lord should wear rich jewels,
And send for money for 'em.

speeches.speech: I'm weary of this charge, the gods can witness: I know my lord hath spent of Timon's wealth, And now ingratitude makes it worse than stealth.
lines: I'm weary of this charge, the gods can witness:
I know my lord hath spent of Timon's wealth,
And now ingratitude makes it worse than stealth.

speeches.speech: Yes, mine's three thousand crowns: what's yours?
lines: Yes, mine's three thousand crowns: what's yours?

speeches.speech: 'Tis much deep: and it should seem by the sun, Your master's confidence was above mine; Else, surely, his had equall'd. Enter Flaminius.
lines: 'Tis much deep: and it should seem by the sun,
Your master's confidence was above mine;
Else, surely, his had equall'd.
Enter Flaminius.

speeches.speech: One of Lord Timon's men. come forth?
lines: One of Lord Timon's men.
come forth?

speeches.speech: No, indeed, he is not.
lines: No, indeed, he is not.

speeches.speech: We attend his lordship; pray, signify so much.
lines: We attend his lordship; pray, signify so much.

speeches.speech: I need not tell him that; he knows you are too diligent.
lines: I need not tell him that; he knows you are too diligent.

speeches.speech: Do you hear, sir?
lines: Do you hear, sir?

speeches.speech: By your leave, sir,--
lines: By your leave, sir,--

speeches.speech: What do ye ask of me, my friend?
lines: What do ye ask of me, my friend?

speeches.speech: We wait for certain money here, sir.
lines: We wait for certain money here, sir.

speeches.speech: Ay, If money were as certain as your waiting, 'Twere sure enough. Why then preferr'd you not your sums and bills, When your false masters eat of my lord's meat? Then they could smile and fawn upon his debts And take down the interest into their gluttonous maws. You do yourselves but wrong to stir me up; Let me pass quietly: Believe 't, my lord and I have made an end; I have no more to reckon, he to spend.
lines: Ay,
If money were as certain as your waiting,
'Twere sure enough.
Why then preferr'd you not your sums and bills,
When your false masters eat of my lord's meat?
Then they could smile and fawn upon his debts
And take down the interest into their
gluttonous maws.
You do yourselves but wrong to stir me up;
Let me pass quietly:
Believe 't, my lord and I have made an end;
I have no more to reckon, he to spend.

speeches.speech: If 'twill not serve,'tis not so base as you; For you serve knaves.
lines: If 'twill not serve,'tis not so base as you;
For you serve knaves.

speeches.speech: How! what does his cashiered worship mutter?
lines: How! what does his cashiered worship mutter?

speeches.speech: No matter what; he's poor, and that's revenge enough. Who can speak broader than he that has no house to put his head in? such may rail against great buildings.
lines: No matter what; he's poor, and that's revenge
enough. Who can speak broader than he that has no
house to put his head in? such may rail against
great buildings.

speeches.speech: O, here's Servilius; now we shall know some answer.
lines: O, here's Servilius; now we shall know some answer.

speeches.speech: If I might beseech you, gentlemen, to repair some other hour, I should derive much from't; for, take't of my soul, my lord leans wondrously to discontent: his comfortable temper has forsook him; he's much out of health, and keeps his chamber. And, if it be so far beyond his health, Methinks he should the sooner pay his debts, And make a clear way to the gods.
lines: If I might beseech you, gentlemen, to repair some
other hour, I should derive much from't; for,
take't of my soul, my lord leans wondrously to
discontent: his comfortable temper has forsook him;
he's much out of health, and keeps his chamber.
And, if it be so far beyond his health,
Methinks he should the sooner pay his debts,
And make a clear way to the gods.

speeches.speech: Good gods!
lines: Good gods!

speeches.speech: We cannot take this for answer, sir.
lines: We cannot take this for answer, sir.

speeches.speech: [Within] Servilius, help! My lord! my lord!
lines: [Within] Servilius, help! My lord! my lord!

speeches.speech: What, are my doors opposed against my passage? Have I been ever free, and must my house Be my retentive enemy, my gaol? The place which I have feasted, does it now, Like all mankind, show me an iron heart?
lines: What, are my doors opposed against my passage?
Have I been ever free, and must my house
Be my retentive enemy, my gaol?
The place which I have feasted, does it now,
Like all mankind, show me an iron heart?

speeches.speech: My lord, here is my bill.
lines: My lord, here is my bill.

speeches.speech: And mine, my lord.
lines: And mine, my lord.

speeches.speech: All our bills.
lines: All our bills.

speeches.speech: Knock me down with 'em: cleave me to the girdle.
lines: Knock me down with 'em: cleave me to the girdle.

speeches.speech: Cut my heart in sums.
lines: Cut my heart in sums.

speeches.speech: Mine, fifty talents.
lines: Mine, fifty talents.

speeches.speech: Tell out my blood.
lines: Tell out my blood.

speeches.speech: Five thousand drops pays that. What yours?--and yours?
lines: Five thousand drops pays that.
What yours?--and yours?

speeches.speech: My lord,--
lines: My lord,--

speeches.speech: My lord,--
lines: My lord,--

speeches.speech: Tear me, take me, and the gods fall upon you!
lines: Tear me, take me, and the gods fall upon you!

speeches.speech: 'Faith, I perceive our masters may throw their caps at their money: these debts may well be called desperate ones, for a madman owes 'em.
lines: 'Faith, I perceive our masters may throw their caps
at their money: these debts may well be called
desperate ones, for a madman owes 'em.

speeches.speech: They have e'en put my breath from me, the slaves. Creditors? devils!
lines: They have e'en put my breath from me, the slaves.
Creditors? devils!

speeches.speech: My dear lord,--
lines: My dear lord,--

speeches.speech: What if it should be so?
lines: What if it should be so?

speeches.speech: My lord,--
lines: My lord,--

speeches.speech: I'll have it so. My steward!
lines: I'll have it so. My steward!

speeches.speech: Here, my lord.
lines: Here, my lord.

speeches.speech: So fitly? Go, bid all my friends again, Lucius, Lucullus, and Sempronius: All, sirrah, all: I'll once more feast the rascals.
lines: So fitly? Go, bid all my friends again,
Lucius, Lucullus, and Sempronius:
All, sirrah, all:
I'll once more feast the rascals.

speeches.speech: O my lord, You only speak from your distracted soul; There is not so much left, to furnish out A moderate table.
lines: O my lord,
You only speak from your distracted soul;
There is not so much left, to furnish out
A moderate table.

speeches.speech: Be't not in thy care; go, I charge thee, invite them all: let in the tide Of knaves once more; my cook and I'll provide.
lines: Be't not in thy care; go,
I charge thee, invite them all: let in the tide
Of knaves once more; my cook and I'll provide.

speeches.speech: My lord, you have my voice to it; the fault's Bloody; 'tis necessary he should die: Nothing emboldens sin so much as mercy.
lines: My lord, you have my voice to it; the fault's
Bloody; 'tis necessary he should die:
Nothing emboldens sin so much as mercy.

speeches.speech: Most true; the law shall bruise him.
lines: Most true; the law shall bruise him.

speeches.speech: Honour, health, and compassion to the senate!
lines: Honour, health, and compassion to the senate!

speeches.speech: Now, captain?
lines: Now, captain?

speeches.speech: I am an humble suitor to your virtues; For pity is the virtue of the law, And none but tyrants use it cruelly. It pleases time and fortune to lie heavy Upon a friend of mine, who, in hot blood, Hath stepp'd into the law, which is past depth To those that, without heed, do plunge into 't. He is a man, setting his fate aside, Of comely virtues: Nor did he soil the fact with cowardice-- An honour in him which buys out his fault-- But with a noble fury and fair spirit, Seeing his reputation touch'd to death, He did oppose his foe: And with such sober and unnoted passion He did behave his anger, ere 'twas spent, As if he had but proved an argument.
lines: I am an humble suitor to your virtues;
For pity is the virtue of the law,
And none but tyrants use it cruelly.
It pleases time and fortune to lie heavy
Upon a friend of mine, who, in hot blood,
Hath stepp'd into the law, which is past depth
To those that, without heed, do plunge into 't.
He is a man, setting his fate aside,
Of comely virtues:
Nor did he soil the fact with cowardice--
An honour in him which buys out his fault--
But with a noble fury and fair spirit,
Seeing his reputation touch'd to death,
He did oppose his foe:
And with such sober and unnoted passion
He did behave his anger, ere 'twas spent,
As if he had but proved an argument.

speeches.speech: You undergo too strict a paradox, Striving to make an ugly deed look fair: Your words have took such pains as if they labour'd To bring manslaughter into form and set quarrelling Upon the head of valour; which indeed Is valour misbegot and came into the world When sects and factions were newly born: He's truly valiant that can wisely suffer The worst that man can breathe, and make his wrongs His outsides, to wear them like his raiment, carelessly, And ne'er prefer his injuries to his heart, To bring it into danger. If wrongs be evils and enforce us kill, What folly 'tis to hazard life for ill!
lines: You undergo too strict a paradox,
Striving to make an ugly deed look fair:
Your words have took such pains as if they labour'd
To bring manslaughter into form and set quarrelling
Upon the head of valour; which indeed
Is valour misbegot and came into the world
When sects and factions were newly born:
He's truly valiant that can wisely suffer
The worst that man can breathe, and make his wrongs
His outsides, to wear them like his raiment,
carelessly,
And ne'er prefer his injuries to his heart,
To bring it into danger.
If wrongs be evils and enforce us kill,
What folly 'tis to hazard life for ill!

speeches.speech: My lord,--
lines: My lord,--

speeches.speech: You cannot make gross sins look clear: To revenge is no valour, but to bear.
lines: You cannot make gross sins look clear:
To revenge is no valour, but to bear.

speeches.speech: My lords, then, under favour, pardon me, If I speak like a captain. Why do fond men expose themselves to battle, And not endure all threats? sleep upon't, And let the foes quietly cut their throats, Without repugnancy? If there be Such valour in the bearing, what make we Abroad? why then, women are more valiant That stay at home, if bearing carry it, And the ass more captain than the lion, the felon Loaden with irons wiser than the judge, If wisdom be in suffering. O my lords, As you are great, be pitifully good: Who cannot condemn rashness in cold blood? To kill, I grant, is sin's extremest gust; But, in defence, by mercy, 'tis most just. To be in anger is impiety; But who is man that is not angry? Weigh but the crime with this.
lines: My lords, then, under favour, pardon me,
If I speak like a captain.
Why do fond men expose themselves to battle,
And not endure all threats? sleep upon't,
And let the foes quietly cut their throats,
Without repugnancy? If there be
Such valour in the bearing, what make we
Abroad? why then, women are more valiant
That stay at home, if bearing carry it,
And the ass more captain than the lion, the felon
Loaden with irons wiser than the judge,
If wisdom be in suffering. O my lords,
As you are great, be pitifully good:
Who cannot condemn rashness in cold blood?
To kill, I grant, is sin's extremest gust;
But, in defence, by mercy, 'tis most just.
To be in anger is impiety;
But who is man that is not angry?
Weigh but the crime with this.

speeches.speech: You breathe in vain.
lines: You breathe in vain.

speeches.speech: In vain! his service done At Lacedaemon and Byzantium Were a sufficient briber for his life.
lines: In vain! his service done
At Lacedaemon and Byzantium
Were a sufficient briber for his life.

speeches.speech: What's that?
lines: What's that?

speeches.speech: I say, my lords, he has done fair service, And slain in fight many of your enemies: How full of valour did he bear himself In the last conflict, and made plenteous wounds!
lines: I say, my lords, he has done fair service,
And slain in fight many of your enemies:
How full of valour did he bear himself
In the last conflict, and made plenteous wounds!

speeches.speech: He has made too much plenty with 'em; He's a sworn rioter: he has a sin that often Drowns him, and takes his valour prisoner: If there were no foes, that were enough To overcome him: in that beastly fury He has been known to commit outrages, And cherish factions: 'tis inferr'd to us, His days are foul and his drink dangerous.
lines: He has made too much plenty with 'em;
He's a sworn rioter: he has a sin that often
Drowns him, and takes his valour prisoner:
If there were no foes, that were enough
To overcome him: in that beastly fury
He has been known to commit outrages,
And cherish factions: 'tis inferr'd to us,
His days are foul and his drink dangerous.

speeches.speech: He dies.
lines: He dies.

speeches.speech: Hard fate! he might have died in war. My lords, if not for any parts in him-- Though his right arm might purchase his own time And be in debt to none--yet, more to move you, Take my deserts to his, and join 'em both: And, for I know your reverend ages love Security, I'll pawn my victories, all My honours to you, upon his good returns. If by this crime he owes the law his life, Why, let the war receive 't in valiant gore For law is strict, and war is nothing more.
lines: Hard fate! he might have died in war.
My lords, if not for any parts in him--
Though his right arm might purchase his own time
And be in debt to none--yet, more to move you,
Take my deserts to his, and join 'em both:
And, for I know your reverend ages love
Security, I'll pawn my victories, all
My honours to you, upon his good returns.
If by this crime he owes the law his life,
Why, let the war receive 't in valiant gore
For law is strict, and war is nothing more.

speeches.speech: We are for law: he dies; urge it no more, On height of our displeasure: friend or brother, He forfeits his own blood that spills another.
lines: We are for law: he dies; urge it no more,
On height of our displeasure: friend or brother,
He forfeits his own blood that spills another.

speeches.speech: Must it be so? it must not be. My lords, I do beseech you, know me.
lines: Must it be so? it must not be. My lords,
I do beseech you, know me.

speeches.speech: How!
lines: How!

speeches.speech: Call me to your remembrances.
lines: Call me to your remembrances.

speeches.speech: What!
lines: What!

speeches.speech: I cannot think but your age has forgot me; It could not else be, I should prove so base, To sue, and be denied such common grace: My wounds ache at you.
lines: I cannot think but your age has forgot me;
It could not else be, I should prove so base,
To sue, and be denied such common grace:
My wounds ache at you.

speeches.speech: Do you dare our anger? 'Tis in few words, but spacious in effect; We banish thee for ever.
lines: Do you dare our anger?
'Tis in few words, but spacious in effect;
We banish thee for ever.

speeches.speech: Banish me! Banish your dotage; banish usury, That makes the senate ugly.
lines: Banish me!
Banish your dotage; banish usury,
That makes the senate ugly.

speeches.speech: If, after two days' shine, Athens contain thee, Attend our weightier judgment. And, not to swell our spirit, He shall be executed presently.
lines: If, after two days' shine, Athens contain thee,
Attend our weightier judgment. And, not to swell
our spirit,
He shall be executed presently.

speeches.speech: Now the gods keep you old enough; that you may live Only in bone, that none may look on you! I'm worse than mad: I have kept back their foes, While they have told their money and let out Their coin upon large interest, I myself Rich only in large hurts. All those for this? Is this the balsam that the usuring senate Pours into captains' wounds? Banishment! It comes not ill; I hate not to be banish'd; It is a cause worthy my spleen and fury, That I may strike at Athens. I'll cheer up My discontented troops, and lay for hearts. 'Tis honour with most lands to be at odds; Soldiers should brook as little wrongs as gods.
lines: Now the gods keep you old enough; that you may live
Only in bone, that none may look on you!
I'm worse than mad: I have kept back their foes,
While they have told their money and let out
Their coin upon large interest, I myself
Rich only in large hurts. All those for this?
Is this the balsam that the usuring senate
Pours into captains' wounds? Banishment!
It comes not ill; I hate not to be banish'd;
It is a cause worthy my spleen and fury,
That I may strike at Athens. I'll cheer up
My discontented troops, and lay for hearts.
'Tis honour with most lands to be at odds;
Soldiers should brook as little wrongs as gods.

speeches.speech: The good time of day to you, sir.
lines: The good time of day to you, sir.

speeches.speech: I also wish it to you. I think this honourable lord did but try us this other day.
lines: I also wish it to you. I think this honourable lord
did but try us this other day.

speeches.speech: Upon that were my thoughts tiring, when we encountered: I hope it is not so low with him as he made it seem in the trial of his several friends.
lines: Upon that were my thoughts tiring, when we
encountered: I hope it is not so low with him as
he made it seem in the trial of his several friends.

speeches.speech: It should not be, by the persuasion of his new feasting.
lines: It should not be, by the persuasion of his new feasting.

speeches.speech: I should think so: he hath sent me an earnest inviting, which many my near occasions did urge me to put off; but he hath conjured me beyond them, and I must needs appear.
lines: I should think so: he hath sent me an earnest
inviting, which many my near occasions did urge me
to put off; but he hath conjured me beyond them, and
I must needs appear.

speeches.speech: In like manner was I in debt to my importunate business, but he would not hear my excuse. I am sorry, when he sent to borrow of me, that my provision was out.
lines: In like manner was I in debt to my importunate
business, but he would not hear my excuse. I am
sorry, when he sent to borrow of me, that my
provision was out.

speeches.speech: I am sick of that grief too, as I understand how all things go.
lines: I am sick of that grief too, as I understand how all
things go.

speeches.speech: Every man here's so. What would he have borrowed of you?
lines: Every man here's so. What would he have borrowed of
you?

speeches.speech: A thousand pieces.
lines: A thousand pieces.

speeches.speech: A thousand pieces!
lines: A thousand pieces!

speeches.speech: What of you?
lines: What of you?

speeches.speech: He sent to me, sir,--Here he comes.
lines: He sent to me, sir,--Here he comes.

speeches.speech: With all my heart, gentlemen both; and how fare you?
lines: With all my heart, gentlemen both; and how fare you?

speeches.speech: Ever at the best, hearing well of your lordship.
lines: Ever at the best, hearing well of your lordship.

speeches.speech: The swallow follows not summer more willing than we your lordship.
lines: The swallow follows not summer more willing than we
your lordship.

speeches.speech: [Aside] Nor more willingly leaves winter; such summer-birds are men. Gentlemen, our dinner will not recompense this long stay: feast your ears with the music awhile, if they will fare so harshly o' the trumpet's sound; we shall to 't presently.
lines: [Aside] Nor more willingly leaves winter; such
summer-birds are men. Gentlemen, our dinner will not
recompense this long stay: feast your ears with the
music awhile, if they will fare so harshly o' the
trumpet's sound; we shall to 't presently.

speeches.speech: I hope it remains not unkindly with your lordship that I returned you an empty messenger.
lines: I hope it remains not unkindly with your lordship
that I returned you an empty messenger.

speeches.speech: O, sir, let it not trouble you.
lines: O, sir, let it not trouble you.

speeches.speech: My noble lord,--
lines: My noble lord,--

speeches.speech: Ah, my good friend, what cheer?
lines: Ah, my good friend, what cheer?

speeches.speech: My most honourable lord, I am e'en sick of shame, that, when your lordship this other day sent to me, I was so unfortunate a beggar.
lines: My most honourable lord, I am e'en sick of shame,
that, when your lordship this other day sent to me,
I was so unfortunate a beggar.

speeches.speech: Think not on 't, sir.
lines: Think not on 't, sir.

speeches.speech: If you had sent but two hours before,--
lines: If you had sent but two hours before,--

speeches.speech: Let it not cumber your better remembrance. [The banquet brought in] Come, bring in all together.
lines: Let it not cumber your better remembrance.
[The banquet brought in]
Come, bring in all together.

speeches.speech: All covered dishes!
lines: All covered dishes!

speeches.speech: Royal cheer, I warrant you.
lines: Royal cheer, I warrant you.

speeches.speech: Doubt not that, if money and the season can yield it.
lines: Doubt not that, if money and the season can yield
it.

speeches.speech: How do you? What's the news?
lines: How do you? What's the news?

speeches.speech: Alcibiades is banished: hear you of it?
lines: Alcibiades is banished: hear you of it?

speeches.speech: [with Second Lord] Alcibiades banished!
lines: [with Second Lord] Alcibiades banished!

speeches.speech: 'Tis so, be sure of it.
lines: 'Tis so, be sure of it.

speeches.speech: How! how!
lines: How! how!

speeches.speech: I pray you, upon what?
lines: I pray you, upon what?

speeches.speech: My worthy friends, will you draw near?
lines: My worthy friends, will you draw near?

speeches.speech: I'll tell you more anon. Here's a noble feast toward.
lines: I'll tell you more anon. Here's a noble feast toward.

speeches.speech: This is the old man still.
lines: This is the old man still.

speeches.speech: Will 't hold? will 't hold?
lines: Will 't hold? will 't hold?

speeches.speech: It does: but time will--and so--
lines: It does: but time will--and so--

speeches.speech: I do conceive.
lines: I do conceive.

speeches.speech: Each man to his stool, with that spur as he would to the lip of his mistress: your diet shall be in all places alike. Make not a city feast of it, to let the meat cool ere we can agree upon the first place: sit, sit. The gods require our thanks. You great benefactors, sprinkle our society with thankfulness. For your own gifts, make yourselves praised: but reserve still to give, lest your deities be despised. Lend to each man enough, that one need not lend to another; for, were your godheads to borrow of men, men would forsake the gods. Make the meat be beloved more than the man that gives it. Let no assembly of twenty be without a score of villains: if there sit twelve women at the table, let a dozen of them be--as they are. The rest of your fees, O gods--the senators of Athens, together with the common lag of people--what is amiss in them, you gods, make suitable for destruction. For these my present friends, as they are to me nothing, so in nothing bless them, and to nothing are they welcome. Uncover, dogs, and lap. [The dishes are uncovered and seen to be full of] warm water]
lines: Each man to his stool, with that spur as he would to
the lip of his mistress: your diet shall be in all
places alike. Make not a city feast of it, to let

sit, sit. The gods require our thanks.
You great benefactors, sprinkle our society with
thankfulness. For your own gifts, make yourselves
praised: but reserve still to give, lest your
deities be despised. Lend to each man enough, that
one need not lend to another; for, were your
godheads to borrow of men, men would forsake the
gods. Make the meat be beloved more than the man
that gives it. Let no assembly of twenty be without
a score of villains: if there sit twelve women at
the table, let a dozen of them be--as they are. The

warm water]

speeches.speech: What does his lordship mean?
lines: What does his lordship mean?

speeches.speech: I know not.
lines: I know not.

speeches.speech: May you a better feast never behold, You knot of mouth-friends I smoke and lukewarm water Is your perfection. This is Timon's last; Who, stuck and spangled with your flatteries, Washes it off, and sprinkles in your faces Your reeking villany. [Throwing the water in their faces] Live loathed and long, Most smiling, smooth, detested parasites, Courteous destroyers, affable wolves, meek bears, You fools of fortune, trencher-friends, time's flies, Cap and knee slaves, vapours, and minute-jacks! Of man and beast the infinite malady Crust you quite o'er! What, dost thou go? Soft! take thy physic first--thou too--and thou;-- Stay, I will lend thee money, borrow none. [Throws the dishes at them, and drives them out] What, all in motion? Henceforth be no feast, Whereat a villain's not a welcome guest. Burn, house! sink, Athens! henceforth hated be Of Timon man and all humanity!
lines: May you a better feast never behold,
You knot of mouth-friends I smoke and lukewarm water
Is your perfection. This is Timon's last;
Who, stuck and spangled with your flatteries,
Washes it off, and sprinkles in your faces
Your reeking villany.
[Throwing the water in their faces]
Live loathed and long,
Most smiling, smooth, detested parasites,
Courteous destroyers, affable wolves, meek bears,
You fools of fortune, trencher-friends, time's flies,
Cap and knee slaves, vapours, and minute-jacks!
Of man and beast the infinite malady
Crust you quite o'er! What, dost thou go?
Soft! take thy physic first--thou too--and thou;--
Stay, I will lend thee money, borrow none.
[Throws the dishes at them, and drives them out]
What, all in motion? Henceforth be no feast,
Whereat a villain's not a welcome guest.
Burn, house! sink, Athens! henceforth hated be
Of Timon man and all humanity!

speeches.speech: How now, my lords!
lines: How now, my lords!

speeches.speech: Know you the quality of Lord Timon's fury?
lines: Know you the quality of Lord Timon's fury?

speeches.speech: Push! did you see my cap?
lines: Push! did you see my cap?

speeches.speech: I have lost my gown.
lines: I have lost my gown.

speeches.speech: He's but a mad lord, and nought but humour sways him. He gave me a jewel th' other day, and now he has beat it out of my hat: did you see my jewel?
lines: He's but a mad lord, and nought but humour sways him.
He gave me a jewel th' other day, and now he has
beat it out of my hat: did you see my jewel?

speeches.speech: Did you see my cap?
lines: Did you see my cap?

speeches.speech: Here 'tis.
lines: Here 'tis.

speeches.speech: Here lies my gown.
lines: Here lies my gown.

speeches.speech: Let's make no stay.
lines: Let's make no stay.

speeches.speech: Lord Timon's mad.
lines: Lord Timon's mad.

speeches.speech: I feel 't upon my bones.
lines: I feel 't upon my bones.

speeches.speech: One day he gives us diamonds, next day stones.
lines: One day he gives us diamonds, next day stones.

speeches.speech: Let me look back upon thee. O thou wall, That girdlest in those wolves, dive in the earth, And fence not Athens! Matrons, turn incontinent! Obedience fail in children! slaves and fools, Pluck the grave wrinkled senate from the bench, And minister in their steads! to general filths Convert o' the instant, green virginity, Do 't in your parents' eyes! bankrupts, hold fast; Rather than render back, out with your knives, And cut your trusters' throats! bound servants, steal! Large-handed robbers your grave masters are, And pill by law. Maid, to thy master's bed; Thy mistress is o' the brothel! Son of sixteen, pluck the lined crutch from thy old limping sire, With it beat out his brains! Piety, and fear, Religion to the gods, peace, justice, truth, Domestic awe, night-rest, and neighbourhood, Instruction, manners, mysteries, and trades, Degrees, observances, customs, and laws, Decline to your confounding contraries, And let confusion live! Plagues, incident to men, Your potent and infectious fevers heap On Athens, ripe for stroke! Thou cold sciatica, Cripple our senators, that their limbs may halt As lamely as their manners. Lust and liberty Creep in the minds and marrows of our youth, That 'gainst the stream of virtue they may strive, And drown themselves in riot! Itches, blains, Sow all the Athenian bosoms; and their crop Be general leprosy! Breath infect breath, at their society, as their friendship, may merely poison! Nothing I'll bear from thee, But nakedness, thou detestable town! Take thou that too, with multiplying bans! Timon will to the woods; where he shall find The unkindest beast more kinder than mankind. The gods confound--hear me, you good gods all-- The Athenians both within and out that wall! And grant, as Timon grows, his hate may grow To the whole race of mankind, high and low! Amen.
lines: Let me look back upon thee. O thou wall,
That girdlest in those wolves, dive in the earth,
And fence not Athens! Matrons, turn incontinent!
Obedience fail in children! slaves and fools,
Pluck the grave wrinkled senate from the bench,
And minister in their steads! to general filths
Convert o' the instant, green virginity,
Do 't in your parents' eyes! bankrupts, hold fast;
Rather than render back, out with your knives,
And cut your trusters' throats! bound servants, steal!
Large-handed robbers your grave masters are,
And pill by law. Maid, to thy master's bed;
Thy mistress is o' the brothel! Son of sixteen,
pluck the lined crutch from thy old limping sire,
With it beat out his brains! Piety, and fear,
Religion to the gods, peace, justice, truth,
Domestic awe, night-rest, and neighbourhood,
Instruction, manners, mysteries, and trades,
Degrees, observances, customs, and laws,
Decline to your confounding contraries,
And let confusion live! Plagues, incident to men,
Your potent and infectious fevers heap
On Athens, ripe for stroke! Thou cold sciatica,
Cripple our senators, that their limbs may halt
As lamely as their manners. Lust and liberty
Creep in the minds and marrows of our youth,
That 'gainst the stream of virtue they may strive,
And drown themselves in riot! Itches, blains,
Sow all the Athenian bosoms; and their crop
Be general leprosy! Breath infect breath,
at their society, as their friendship, may
merely poison! Nothing I'll bear from thee,
But nakedness, thou detestable town!
Take thou that too, with multiplying bans!
Timon will to the woods; where he shall find
The unkindest beast more kinder than mankind.
The gods confound--hear me, you good gods all--
The Athenians both within and out that wall!
And grant, as Timon grows, his hate may grow
To the whole race of mankind, high and low! Amen.

speeches.speech: Hear you, master steward, where's our master? Are we undone? cast off? nothing remaining?
lines: Hear you, master steward, where's our master?
Are we undone? cast off? nothing remaining?

speeches.speech: Alack, my fellows, what should I say to you? Let me be recorded by the righteous gods, I am as poor as you.
lines: Alack, my fellows, what should I say to you?
Let me be recorded by the righteous gods,
I am as poor as you.

speeches.speech: Such a house broke! So noble a master fall'n! All gone! and not One friend to take his fortune by the arm, And go along with him!
lines: Such a house broke!
So noble a master fall'n! All gone! and not
One friend to take his fortune by the arm,
And go along with him!

speeches.speech: As we do turn our backs From our companion thrown into his grave, So his familiars to his buried fortunes Slink all away, leave their false vows with him, Like empty purses pick'd; and his poor self, A dedicated beggar to the air, With his disease of all-shunn'd poverty, Walks, like contempt, alone. More of our fellows.
lines: As we do turn our backs
From our companion thrown into his grave,
So his familiars to his buried fortunes
Slink all away, leave their false vows with him,
Like empty purses pick'd; and his poor self,
A dedicated beggar to the air,
With his disease of all-shunn'd poverty,
Walks, like contempt, alone. More of our fellows.

speeches.speech: All broken implements of a ruin'd house.
lines: All broken implements of a ruin'd house.

speeches.speech: Yet do our hearts wear Timon's livery; That see I by our faces; we are fellows still, Serving alike in sorrow: leak'd is our bark, And we, poor mates, stand on the dying deck, Hearing the surges threat: we must all part Into this sea of air.
lines: Yet do our hearts wear Timon's livery;
That see I by our faces; we are fellows still,
Serving alike in sorrow: leak'd is our bark,
And we, poor mates, stand on the dying deck,
Hearing the surges threat: we must all part
Into this sea of air.

speeches.speech: Good fellows all, The latest of my wealth I'll share amongst you. Wherever we shall meet, for Timon's sake, Let's yet be fellows; let's shake our heads, and say, As 'twere a knell unto our master's fortunes, 'We have seen better days.' Let each take some; Nay, put out all your hands. Not one word more: Thus part we rich in sorrow, parting poor. [Servants embrace, and part several ways] O, the fierce wretchedness that glory brings us! Who would not wish to be from wealth exempt, Since riches point to misery and contempt? Who would be so mock'd with glory? or to live But in a dream of friendship? To have his pomp and all what state compounds But only painted, like his varnish'd friends? Poor honest lord, brought low by his own heart, Undone by goodness! Strange, unusual blood, When man's worst sin is, he does too much good! Who, then, dares to be half so kind again? For bounty, that makes gods, does still mar men. My dearest lord, bless'd, to be most accursed, Rich, only to be wretched, thy great fortunes Are made thy chief afflictions. Alas, kind lord! He's flung in rage from this ingrateful seat Of monstrous friends, nor has he with him to Supply his life, or that which can command it. I'll follow and inquire him out: I'll ever serve his mind with my best will; Whilst I have gold, I'll be his steward still.
lines: Good fellows all,
The latest of my wealth I'll share amongst you.
Wherever we shall meet, for Timon's sake,
Let's yet be fellows; let's shake our heads, and say,
As 'twere a knell unto our master's fortunes,
'We have seen better days.' Let each take some;
Nay, put out all your hands. Not one word more:
Thus part we rich in sorrow, parting poor.
[Servants embrace, and part several ways]
O, the fierce wretchedness that glory brings us!
Who would not wish to be from wealth exempt,
Since riches point to misery and contempt?
Who would be so mock'd with glory? or to live
But in a dream of friendship?
To have his pomp and all what state compounds
But only painted, like his varnish'd friends?
Poor honest lord, brought low by his own heart,
Undone by goodness! Strange, unusual blood,
When man's worst sin is, he does too much good!
Who, then, dares to be half so kind again?
For bounty, that makes gods, does still mar men.
My dearest lord, bless'd, to be most accursed,
Rich, only to be wretched, thy great fortunes
Are made thy chief afflictions. Alas, kind lord!
He's flung in rage from this ingrateful seat
Of monstrous friends, nor has he with him to
Supply his life, or that which can command it.
I'll follow and inquire him out:
I'll ever serve his mind with my best will;
Whilst I have gold, I'll be his steward still.

speeches.speech: O blessed breeding sun, draw from the earth Rotten humidity; below thy sister's orb Infect the air! Twinn'd brothers of one womb, Whose procreation, residence, and birth, Scarce is dividant, touch them with several fortunes; The greater scorns the lesser: not nature, To whom all sores lay siege, can bear great fortune, But by contempt of nature. Raise me this beggar, and deny 't that lord; The senator shall bear contempt hereditary, The beggar native honour. It is the pasture lards the rother's sides, The want that makes him lean. Who dares, who dares, In purity of manhood stand upright, And say 'This man's a flatterer?' if one be, So are they all; for every grise of fortune Is smooth'd by that below: the learned pate Ducks to the golden fool: all is oblique; There's nothing level in our cursed natures, But direct villany. Therefore, be abhorr'd All feasts, societies, and throngs of men! His semblable, yea, himself, Timon disdains: Destruction fang mankind! Earth, yield me roots! [Digging] Who seeks for better of thee, sauce his palate With thy most operant poison! What is here? Gold? yellow, glittering, precious gold? No, gods, I am no idle votarist: roots, you clear heavens! Thus much of this will make black white, foul fair, Wrong right, base noble, old young, coward valiant. Ha, you gods! why this? what this, you gods? Why, this Will lug your priests and servants from your sides, Pluck stout men's pillows from below their heads: This yellow slave Will knit and break religions, bless the accursed, Make the hoar leprosy adored, place thieves And give them title, knee and approbation With senators on the bench: this is it That makes the wappen'd widow wed again; She, whom the spital-house and ulcerous sores Would cast the gorge at, this embalms and spices To the April day again. Come, damned earth, Thou common whore of mankind, that put'st odds Among the route of nations, I will make thee Do thy right nature. [March afar off] Ha! a drum? Thou'rt quick, But yet I'll bury thee: thou'lt go, strong thief, When gouty keepers of thee cannot stand. Nay, stay thou out for earnest. [Keeping some gold] [Enter ALCIBIADES, with drum and fife, in] warlike manner; PHRYNIA and TIMANDRA]
lines: O blessed breeding sun, draw from the earth
Rotten humidity; below thy sister's orb
Infect the air! Twinn'd brothers of one womb,
Whose procreation, residence, and birth,
Scarce is dividant, touch them with several fortunes;
The greater scorns the lesser: not nature,
To whom all sores lay siege, can bear great fortune,
But by contempt of nature.
Raise me this beggar, and deny 't that lord;
The senator shall bear contempt hereditary,
The beggar native honour.
It is the pasture lards the rother's sides,
The want that makes him lean. Who dares, who dares,
In purity of manhood stand upright,
And say 'This man's a flatterer?' if one be,
So are they all; for every grise of fortune
Is smooth'd by that below: the learned pate
Ducks to the golden fool: all is oblique;
There's nothing level in our cursed natures,
But direct villany. Therefore, be abhorr'd
All feasts, societies, and throngs of men!
His semblable, yea, himself, Timon disdains:
Destruction fang mankind! Earth, yield me roots!
[Digging]
Who seeks for better of thee, sauce his palate
With thy most operant poison! What is here?
Gold? yellow, glittering, precious gold? No, gods,
I am no idle votarist: roots, you clear heavens!
Thus much of this will make black white, foul fair,
Wrong right, base noble, old young, coward valiant.
Ha, you gods! why this? what this, you gods? Why, this
Will lug your priests and servants from your sides,
Pluck stout men's pillows from below their heads:
This yellow slave
Will knit and break religions, bless the accursed,
Make the hoar leprosy adored, place thieves
And give them title, knee and approbation
With senators on the bench: this is it
That makes the wappen'd widow wed again;
She, whom the spital-house and ulcerous sores
Would cast the gorge at, this embalms and spices
To the April day again. Come, damned earth,
Thou common whore of mankind, that put'st odds
Among the route of nations, I will make thee
Do thy right nature.
[March afar off]
Ha! a drum? Thou'rt quick,
But yet I'll bury thee: thou'lt go, strong thief,
When gouty keepers of thee cannot stand.
Nay, stay thou out for earnest.
[Keeping some gold]
[Enter ALCIBIADES, with drum and fife, in]
warlike manner; PHRYNIA and TIMANDRA]

speeches.speech: What art thou there? speak.
lines: What art thou there? speak.

speeches.speech: A beast, as thou art. The canker gnaw thy heart, For showing me again the eyes of man!
lines: A beast, as thou art. The canker gnaw thy heart,
For showing me again the eyes of man!

speeches.speech: What is thy name? Is man so hateful to thee, That art thyself a man?
lines: What is thy name? Is man so hateful to thee,
That art thyself a man?

speeches.speech: I am Misanthropos, and hate mankind. For thy part, I do wish thou wert a dog, That I might love thee something.
lines: I am Misanthropos, and hate mankind.
For thy part, I do wish thou wert a dog,
That I might love thee something.

speeches.speech: I know thee well; But in thy fortunes am unlearn'd and strange.
lines: I know thee well;
But in thy fortunes am unlearn'd and strange.

speeches.speech: I know thee too; and more than that I know thee, I not desire to know. Follow thy drum; With man's blood paint the ground, gules, gules: Religious canons, civil laws are cruel; Then what should war be? This fell whore of thine Hath in her more destruction than thy sword, For all her cherubim look.
lines: I know thee too; and more than that I know thee,
I not desire to know. Follow thy drum;
With man's blood paint the ground, gules, gules:
Religious canons, civil laws are cruel;
Then what should war be? This fell whore of thine
Hath in her more destruction than thy sword,
For all her cherubim look.

speeches.speech: Thy lips rot off!
lines: Thy lips rot off!

speeches.speech: I will not kiss thee; then the rot returns To thine own lips again.
lines: I will not kiss thee; then the rot returns
To thine own lips again.

speeches.speech: How came the noble Timon to this change?
lines: How came the noble Timon to this change?

speeches.speech: As the moon does, by wanting light to give: But then renew I could not, like the moon; There were no suns to borrow of.
lines: As the moon does, by wanting light to give:
But then renew I could not, like the moon;
There were no suns to borrow of.

speeches.speech: Noble Timon, What friendship may I do thee?
lines: Noble Timon,
What friendship may I do thee?

speeches.speech: None, but to Maintain my opinion.
lines: None, but to
Maintain my opinion.

speeches.speech: What is it, Timon?
lines: What is it, Timon?

speeches.speech: Promise me friendship, but perform none: if thou wilt not promise, the gods plague thee, for thou art a man! if thou dost perform, confound thee, for thou art a man!
lines: Promise me friendship, but perform none: if thou
wilt not promise, the gods plague thee, for thou art
a man! if thou dost perform, confound thee, for
thou art a man!

speeches.speech: I have heard in some sort of thy miseries.
lines: I have heard in some sort of thy miseries.

speeches.speech: Thou saw'st them, when I had prosperity.
lines: Thou saw'st them, when I had prosperity.

speeches.speech: I see them now; then was a blessed time.
lines: I see them now; then was a blessed time.

speeches.speech: As thine is now, held with a brace of harlots.
lines: As thine is now, held with a brace of harlots.

speeches.speech: Is this the Athenian minion, whom the world Voiced so regardfully?
lines: Is this the Athenian minion, whom the world
Voiced so regardfully?

speeches.speech: Art thou Timandra?
lines: Art thou Timandra?

speeches.speech: Yes.
lines: Yes.

speeches.speech: Be a whore still: they love thee not that use thee; Give them diseases, leaving with thee their lust. Make use of thy salt hours: season the slaves For tubs and baths; bring down rose-cheeked youth To the tub-fast and the diet.
lines: Be a whore still: they love thee not that use thee;
Give them diseases, leaving with thee their lust.
Make use of thy salt hours: season the slaves
For tubs and baths; bring down rose-cheeked youth
To the tub-fast and the diet.

speeches.speech: Hang thee, monster!
lines: Hang thee, monster!

speeches.speech: Pardon him, sweet Timandra; for his wits Are drown'd and lost in his calamities. I have but little gold of late, brave Timon, The want whereof doth daily make revolt In my penurious band: I have heard, and grieved, How cursed Athens, mindless of thy worth, Forgetting thy great deeds, when neighbour states, But for thy sword and fortune, trod upon them,--
lines: Pardon him, sweet Timandra; for his wits
Are drown'd and lost in his calamities.
I have but little gold of late, brave Timon,
The want whereof doth daily make revolt
In my penurious band: I have heard, and grieved,
How cursed Athens, mindless of thy worth,
Forgetting thy great deeds, when neighbour states,
But for thy sword and fortune, trod upon them,--

speeches.speech: I prithee, beat thy drum, and get thee gone.
lines: I prithee, beat thy drum, and get thee gone.

speeches.speech: I am thy friend, and pity thee, dear Timon.
lines: I am thy friend, and pity thee, dear Timon.

speeches.speech: How dost thou pity him whom thou dost trouble? I had rather be alone.
lines: How dost thou pity him whom thou dost trouble?
I had rather be alone.

speeches.speech: Why, fare thee well: Here is some gold for thee.
lines: Why, fare thee well:
Here is some gold for thee.

speeches.speech: Keep it, I cannot eat it.
lines: Keep it, I cannot eat it.

speeches.speech: When I have laid proud Athens on a heap,--
lines: When I have laid proud Athens on a heap,--

speeches.speech: Warr'st thou 'gainst Athens?
lines: Warr'st thou 'gainst Athens?

speeches.speech: Ay, Timon, and have cause.
lines: Ay, Timon, and have cause.

speeches.speech: The gods confound them all in thy conquest; And thee after, when thou hast conquer'd!
lines: The gods confound them all in thy conquest;
And thee after, when thou hast conquer'd!

speeches.speech: Why me, Timon?
lines: Why me, Timon?

speeches.speech: That, by killing of villains, Thou wast born to conquer my country. Put up thy gold: go on,--here's gold,--go on; Be as a planetary plague, when Jove Will o'er some high-viced city hang his poison In the sick air: let not thy sword skip one: Pity not honour'd age for his white beard; He is an usurer: strike me the counterfeit matron; It is her habit only that is honest, Herself's a bawd: let not the virgin's cheek Make soft thy trenchant sword; for those milk-paps, That through the window-bars bore at men's eyes, Are not within the leaf of pity writ, But set them down horrible traitors: spare not the babe, Whose dimpled smiles from fools exhaust their mercy; Think it a bastard, whom the oracle Hath doubtfully pronounced thy throat shall cut, And mince it sans remorse: swear against objects; Put armour on thine ears and on thine eyes; Whose proof, nor yells of mothers, maids, nor babes, Nor sight of priests in holy vestments bleeding, Shall pierce a jot. There's gold to pay soldiers: Make large confusion; and, thy fury spent, Confounded be thyself! Speak not, be gone.
lines: That, by killing of villains,
Thou wast born to conquer my country.
Put up thy gold: go on,--here's gold,--go on;
Be as a planetary plague, when Jove
Will o'er some high-viced city hang his poison
In the sick air: let not thy sword skip one:
Pity not honour'd age for his white beard;
He is an usurer: strike me the counterfeit matron;
It is her habit only that is honest,
Herself's a bawd: let not the virgin's cheek
Make soft thy trenchant sword; for those milk-paps,
That through the window-bars bore at men's eyes,
Are not within the leaf of pity writ,
But set them down horrible traitors: spare not the babe,
Whose dimpled smiles from fools exhaust their mercy;
Think it a bastard, whom the oracle
Hath doubtfully pronounced thy throat shall cut,
And mince it sans remorse: swear against objects;
Put armour on thine ears and on thine eyes;
Whose proof, nor yells of mothers, maids, nor babes,
Nor sight of priests in holy vestments bleeding,
Shall pierce a jot. There's gold to pay soldiers:
Make large confusion; and, thy fury spent,
Confounded be thyself! Speak not, be gone.

speeches.speech: Hast thou gold yet? I'll take the gold thou givest me, Not all thy counsel.
lines: Hast thou gold yet? I'll take the gold thou
givest me,
Not all thy counsel.

speeches.speech: Dost thou, or dost thou not, heaven's curse upon thee!
lines: Dost thou, or dost thou not, heaven's curse
upon thee!

speeches.speech: [with Timandra] Give us some gold, good Timon: hast thou more?
lines: [with Timandra] Give us some gold, good Timon: hast thou more?

speeches.speech: Enough to make a whore forswear her trade, And to make whores, a bawd. Hold up, you sluts, Your aprons mountant: you are not oathable, Although, I know, you 'll swear, terribly swear Into strong shudders and to heavenly agues The immortal gods that hear you,--spare your oaths, I'll trust to your conditions: be whores still; And he whose pious breath seeks to convert you, Be strong in whore, allure him, burn him up; Let your close fire predominate his smoke, And be no turncoats: yet may your pains, six months, Be quite contrary: and thatch your poor thin roofs With burthens of the dead;--some that were hang'd, No matter:--wear them, betray with them: whore still; Paint till a horse may mire upon your face, A pox of wrinkles!
lines: Enough to make a whore forswear her trade,
And to make whores, a bawd. Hold up, you sluts,
Your aprons mountant: you are not oathable,
Although, I know, you 'll swear, terribly swear
Into strong shudders and to heavenly agues
The immortal gods that hear you,--spare your oaths,
I'll trust to your conditions: be whores still;
And he whose pious breath seeks to convert you,
Be strong in whore, allure him, burn him up;
Let your close fire predominate his smoke,
And be no turncoats: yet may your pains, six months,
Be quite contrary: and thatch your poor thin roofs
With burthens of the dead;--some that were hang'd,
No matter:--wear them, betray with them: whore still;
Paint till a horse may mire upon your face,
A pox of wrinkles!

speeches.speech: [with Timandra] Well, more gold: what then?
lines: [with Timandra] Well, more gold: what then?

speeches.speech: Consumptions sow In hollow bones of man; strike their sharp shins, And mar men's spurring. Crack the lawyer's voice, That he may never more false title plead, Nor sound his quillets shrilly: hoar the flamen, That scolds against the quality of flesh, And not believes himself: down with the nose, Down with it flat; take the bridge quite away Of him that, his particular to foresee, Smells from the general weal: make curl'd-pate ruffians bald; And let the unscarr'd braggarts of the war Derive some pain from you: plague all; That your activity may defeat and quell The source of all erection. There's more gold: Do you damn others, and let this damn you, And ditches grave you all!
lines: Consumptions sow
In hollow bones of man; strike their sharp shins,
And mar men's spurring. Crack the lawyer's voice,
That he may never more false title plead,
Nor sound his quillets shrilly: hoar the flamen,
That scolds against the quality of flesh,
And not believes himself: down with the nose,
Down with it flat; take the bridge quite away
Of him that, his particular to foresee,
Smells from the general weal: make curl'd-pate
ruffians bald;
And let the unscarr'd braggarts of the war
Derive some pain from you: plague all;
That your activity may defeat and quell
The source of all erection. There's more gold:
Do you damn others, and let this damn you,
And ditches grave you all!

speeches.speech: [with Timandra] More counsel with more money, bounteous Timon.
lines: [with Timandra] More counsel with more money, bounteous Timon.

speeches.speech: More whore, more mischief first; I have given you earnest.
lines: More whore, more mischief first; I have given you earnest.

speeches.speech: Strike up the drum towards Athens! Farewell, Timon: If I thrive well, I'll visit thee again.
lines: Strike up the drum towards Athens! Farewell, Timon:
If I thrive well, I'll visit thee again.

speeches.speech: If I hope well, I'll never see thee more.
lines: If I hope well, I'll never see thee more.

speeches.speech: I never did thee harm.
lines: I never did thee harm.

speeches.speech: Yes, thou spokest well of me.
lines: Yes, thou spokest well of me.

speeches.speech: Call'st thou that harm?
lines: Call'st thou that harm?

speeches.speech: Men daily find it. Get thee away, and take Thy beagles with thee.
lines: Men daily find it. Get thee away, and take
Thy beagles with thee.

speeches.speech: We but offend him. Strike! [Drum beats. Exeunt ALCIBIADES, PHRYNIA,] and TIMANDRA]
lines: We but offend him. Strike!
[Drum beats. Exeunt ALCIBIADES, PHRYNIA,]
and TIMANDRA]

speeches.speech: That nature, being sick of man's unkindness, Should yet be hungry! Common mother, thou, [Digging] Whose womb unmeasurable, and infinite breast, Teems, and feeds all; whose self-same mettle, Whereof thy proud child, arrogant man, is puff'd, Engenders the black toad and adder blue, The gilded newt and eyeless venom'd worm, With all the abhorred births below crisp heaven Whereon Hyperion's quickening fire doth shine; Yield him, who all thy human sons doth hate, From forth thy plenteous bosom, one poor root! Ensear thy fertile and conceptious womb, Let it no more bring out ingrateful man! Go great with tigers, dragons, wolves, and bears; Teem with new monsters, whom thy upward face Hath to the marbled mansion all above Never presented!--O, a root,--dear thanks!-- Dry up thy marrows, vines, and plough-torn leas; Whereof ungrateful man, with liquorish draughts And morsels unctuous, greases his pure mind, That from it all consideration slips! [Enter APEMANTUS] More man? plague, plague!
lines: That nature, being sick of man's unkindness,
Should yet be hungry! Common mother, thou,
[Digging]
Whose womb unmeasurable, and infinite breast,
Teems, and feeds all; whose self-same mettle,
Whereof thy proud child, arrogant man, is puff'd,
Engenders the black toad and adder blue,
The gilded newt and eyeless venom'd worm,
With all the abhorred births below crisp heaven
Whereon Hyperion's quickening fire doth shine;
Yield him, who all thy human sons doth hate,
From forth thy plenteous bosom, one poor root!
Ensear thy fertile and conceptious womb,
Let it no more bring out ingrateful man!
Go great with tigers, dragons, wolves, and bears;
Teem with new monsters, whom thy upward face
Hath to the marbled mansion all above
Never presented!--O, a root,--dear thanks!--
Dry up thy marrows, vines, and plough-torn leas;
Whereof ungrateful man, with liquorish draughts
And morsels unctuous, greases his pure mind,
That from it all consideration slips!
[Enter APEMANTUS]
More man? plague, plague!

speeches.speech: I was directed hither: men report Thou dost affect my manners, and dost use them.
lines: I was directed hither: men report
Thou dost affect my manners, and dost use them.

speeches.speech: 'Tis, then, because thou dost not keep a dog, Whom I would imitate: consumption catch thee!
lines: 'Tis, then, because thou dost not keep a dog,
Whom I would imitate: consumption catch thee!

speeches.speech: This is in thee a nature but infected; A poor unmanly melancholy sprung From change of fortune. Why this spade? this place? This slave-like habit? and these looks of care? Thy flatterers yet wear silk, drink wine, lie soft; Hug their diseased perfumes, and have forgot That ever Timon was. Shame not these woods, By putting on the cunning of a carper. Be thou a flatterer now, and seek to thrive By that which has undone thee: hinge thy knee, And let his very breath, whom thou'lt observe, Blow off thy cap; praise his most vicious strain, And call it excellent: thou wast told thus; Thou gavest thine ears like tapsters that bid welcome To knaves and all approachers: 'tis most just That thou turn rascal; hadst thou wealth again, Rascals should have 't. Do not assume my likeness.
lines: This is in thee a nature but infected;
A poor unmanly melancholy sprung
From change of fortune. Why this spade? this place?
This slave-like habit? and these looks of care?
Thy flatterers yet wear silk, drink wine, lie soft;
Hug their diseased perfumes, and have forgot
That ever Timon was. Shame not these woods,
By putting on the cunning of a carper.
Be thou a flatterer now, and seek to thrive
By that which has undone thee: hinge thy knee,
And let his very breath, whom thou'lt observe,
Blow off thy cap; praise his most vicious strain,
And call it excellent: thou wast told thus;
Thou gavest thine ears like tapsters that bid welcome
To knaves and all approachers: 'tis most just
That thou turn rascal; hadst thou wealth again,
Rascals should have 't. Do not assume my likeness.

speeches.speech: Were I like thee, I'ld throw away myself.
lines: Were I like thee, I'ld throw away myself.

speeches.speech: Thou hast cast away thyself, being like thyself; A madman so long, now a fool. What, think'st That the bleak air, thy boisterous chamberlain, Will put thy shirt on warm? will these moss'd trees, That have outlived the eagle, page thy heels, And skip where thou point'st out? will the cold brook, Candied with ice, caudle thy morning taste, To cure thy o'er-night's surfeit? Call the creatures Whose naked natures live in an the spite Of wreakful heaven, whose bare unhoused trunks, To the conflicting elements exposed, Answer mere nature; bid them flatter thee; O, thou shalt find--
lines: Thou hast cast away thyself, being like thyself;
A madman so long, now a fool. What, think'st
That the bleak air, thy boisterous chamberlain,
Will put thy shirt on warm? will these moss'd trees,
That have outlived the eagle, page thy heels,
And skip where thou point'st out? will the
cold brook,
Candied with ice, caudle thy morning taste,
To cure thy o'er-night's surfeit? Call the creatures
Whose naked natures live in an the spite
Of wreakful heaven, whose bare unhoused trunks,
To the conflicting elements exposed,
Answer mere nature; bid them flatter thee;
O, thou shalt find--

speeches.speech: A fool of thee: depart.
lines: A fool of thee: depart.

speeches.speech: I love thee better now than e'er I did.
lines: I love thee better now than e'er I did.

speeches.speech: I hate thee worse.
lines: I hate thee worse.

speeches.speech: Why?
lines: Why?

speeches.speech: Thou flatter'st misery.
lines: Thou flatter'st misery.

speeches.speech: I flatter not; but say thou art a caitiff.
lines: I flatter not; but say thou art a caitiff.

speeches.speech: Why dost thou seek me out?
lines: Why dost thou seek me out?

speeches.speech: To vex thee.
lines: To vex thee.

speeches.speech: Always a villain's office or a fool's. Dost please thyself in't?
lines: Always a villain's office or a fool's.
Dost please thyself in't?

speeches.speech: Ay.
lines: Ay.

speeches.speech: What! a knave too?
lines: What! a knave too?

speeches.speech: If thou didst put this sour-cold habit on To castigate thy pride, 'twere well: but thou Dost it enforcedly; thou'ldst courtier be again, Wert thou not beggar. Willing misery Outlives encertain pomp, is crown'd before: The one is filling still, never complete; The other, at high wish: best state, contentless, Hath a distracted and most wretched being, Worse than the worst, content. Thou shouldst desire to die, being miserable.
lines: If thou didst put this sour-cold habit on
To castigate thy pride, 'twere well: but thou
Dost it enforcedly; thou'ldst courtier be again,
Wert thou not beggar. Willing misery
Outlives encertain pomp, is crown'd before:
The one is filling still, never complete;
The other, at high wish: best state, contentless,
Hath a distracted and most wretched being,
Worse than the worst, content.
Thou shouldst desire to die, being miserable.

speeches.speech: Not by his breath that is more miserable. Thou art a slave, whom Fortune's tender arm With favour never clasp'd; but bred a dog. Hadst thou, like us from our first swath, proceeded The sweet degrees that this brief world affords To such as may the passive drugs of it Freely command, thou wouldst have plunged thyself In general riot; melted down thy youth In different beds of lust; and never learn'd The icy precepts of respect, but follow'd The sugar'd game before thee. But myself, Who had the world as my confectionary, The mouths, the tongues, the eyes and hearts of men At duty, more than I could frame employment, That numberless upon me stuck as leaves Do on the oak, hive with one winter's brush Fell from their boughs and left me open, bare For every storm that blows: I, to bear this, That never knew but better, is some burden: Thy nature did commence in sufferance, time Hath made thee hard in't. Why shouldst thou hate men? They never flatter'd thee: what hast thou given? If thou wilt curse, thy father, that poor rag, Must be thy subject, who in spite put stuff To some she beggar and compounded thee Poor rogue hereditary. Hence, be gone! If thou hadst not been born the worst of men, Thou hadst been a knave and flatterer.
lines: Not by his breath that is more miserable.
Thou art a slave, whom Fortune's tender arm
With favour never clasp'd; but bred a dog.
Hadst thou, like us from our first swath, proceeded
The sweet degrees that this brief world affords
To such as may the passive drugs of it
Freely command, thou wouldst have plunged thyself
In general riot; melted down thy youth
In different beds of lust; and never learn'd
The icy precepts of respect, but follow'd
The sugar'd game before thee. But myself,
Who had the world as my confectionary,
The mouths, the tongues, the eyes and hearts of men
At duty, more than I could frame employment,
That numberless upon me stuck as leaves
Do on the oak, hive with one winter's brush
Fell from their boughs and left me open, bare
For every storm that blows: I, to bear this,
That never knew but better, is some burden:
Thy nature did commence in sufferance, time
Hath made thee hard in't. Why shouldst thou hate men?
They never flatter'd thee: what hast thou given?
If thou wilt curse, thy father, that poor rag,
Must be thy subject, who in spite put stuff
To some she beggar and compounded thee
Poor rogue hereditary. Hence, be gone!
If thou hadst not been born the worst of men,
Thou hadst been a knave and flatterer.

speeches.speech: Art thou proud yet?
lines: Art thou proud yet?

speeches.speech: Ay, that I am not thee.
lines: Ay, that I am not thee.

speeches.speech: I, that I was No prodigal.
lines: I, that I was
No prodigal.

speeches.speech: I, that I am one now: Were all the wealth I have shut up in thee, I'ld give thee leave to hang it. Get thee gone. That the whole life of Athens were in this! Thus would I eat it.
lines: I, that I am one now:
Were all the wealth I have shut up in thee,
I'ld give thee leave to hang it. Get thee gone.
That the whole life of Athens were in this!
Thus would I eat it.

speeches.speech: Here; I will mend thy feast.
lines: Here; I will mend thy feast.

speeches.speech: First mend my company, take away thyself.
lines: First mend my company, take away thyself.

speeches.speech: So I shall mend mine own, by the lack of thine.
lines: So I shall mend mine own, by the lack of thine.

speeches.speech: 'Tis not well mended so, it is but botch'd; if not, I would it were.
lines: 'Tis not well mended so, it is but botch'd;
if not, I would it were.

speeches.speech: What wouldst thou have to Athens?
lines: What wouldst thou have to Athens?

speeches.speech: Thee thither in a whirlwind. If thou wilt, Tell them there I have gold; look, so I have.
lines: Thee thither in a whirlwind. If thou wilt,
Tell them there I have gold; look, so I have.

speeches.speech: Here is no use for gold.
lines: Here is no use for gold.

speeches.speech: The best and truest; For here it sleeps, and does no hired harm.
lines: The best and truest;
For here it sleeps, and does no hired harm.

speeches.speech: Where liest o' nights, Timon?
lines: Where liest o' nights, Timon?

speeches.speech: Under that's above me. Where feed'st thou o' days, Apemantus?
lines: Under that's above me.
Where feed'st thou o' days, Apemantus?

speeches.speech: Where my stomach finds meat; or, rather, where I eat it.
lines: Where my stomach finds meat; or, rather, where I eat
it.

speeches.speech: Would poison were obedient and knew my mind!
lines: Would poison were obedient and knew my mind!

speeches.speech: Where wouldst thou send it?
lines: Where wouldst thou send it?

speeches.speech: To sauce thy dishes.
lines: To sauce thy dishes.

speeches.speech: The middle of humanity thou never knewest, but the extremity of both ends: when thou wast in thy gilt and thy perfume, they mocked thee for too much curiosity; in thy rags thou knowest none, but art despised for the contrary. There's a medlar for thee, eat it.
lines: The middle of humanity thou never knewest, but the
extremity of both ends: when thou wast in thy gilt
and thy perfume, they mocked thee for too much
curiosity; in thy rags thou knowest none, but art
despised for the contrary. There's a medlar for
thee, eat it.

speeches.speech: On what I hate I feed not.
lines: On what I hate I feed not.

speeches.speech: Dost hate a medlar?
lines: Dost hate a medlar?

speeches.speech: Ay, though it look like thee.
lines: Ay, though it look like thee.

speeches.speech: An thou hadst hated meddlers sooner, thou shouldst have loved thyself better now. What man didst thou ever know unthrift that was beloved after his means?
lines: An thou hadst hated meddlers sooner, thou shouldst
have loved thyself better now. What man didst thou
ever know unthrift that was beloved after his means?

speeches.speech: Who, without those means thou talkest of, didst thou ever know beloved?
lines: Who, without those means thou talkest of, didst thou
ever know beloved?

speeches.speech: Myself.
lines: Myself.

speeches.speech: I understand thee; thou hadst some means to keep a dog.
lines: I understand thee; thou hadst some means to keep a
dog.

speeches.speech: What things in the world canst thou nearest compare to thy flatterers?
lines: What things in the world canst thou nearest compare
to thy flatterers?

speeches.speech: Women nearest; but men, men are the things themselves. What wouldst thou do with the world, Apemantus, if it lay in thy power?
lines: Women nearest; but men, men are the things
themselves. What wouldst thou do with the world,
Apemantus, if it lay in thy power?

speeches.speech: Give it the beasts, to be rid of the men.
lines: Give it the beasts, to be rid of the men.

speeches.speech: Wouldst thou have thyself fall in the confusion of men, and remain a beast with the beasts?
lines: Wouldst thou have thyself fall in the confusion of
men, and remain a beast with the beasts?

speeches.speech: Ay, Timon.
lines: Ay, Timon.

speeches.speech: A beastly ambition, which the gods grant thee t' attain to!
